title: Welcome
text:
Welcome
This is the online home of Communicating Uncertainty: A Guide Across Disciplines, authored by Bahman Rostami-Tabar.
Uncertainty is an unavoidable part of knowledge and decision-making in almost every field of human activity, including medicine, finance and economics, supply chains, healthcare, and engineering. However, it is often hidden, oversimplified, or communicated in ways that may mislead the people who rely on this information to make informed decisions.
This book addresses that gap. It provides a practical, cross-disciplinary reference for anyone who needs to communicate uncertainty, whether in a scientific report, a policy document, a public health bulletin, a data visualisation, or a financial forecast. It draws on knowledge from several disciplines, including statistics, psychology, climate science, economics, intelligence analysis, ecology, and risk communication.

title: Welcome
text:
What This Book Covers
The book is organised in three parts:
Part I — Foundations introduces the conceptual landscape. It defines uncertainty and its key frameworks, examines the sources from which uncertainty arises, and situates uncertainty among closely related concepts — variability, volatility, randomness, and risk.
Part II — Why and To Whom addresses the fundamental question of whether and why uncertainty should be communicated at all. It documents the consequences of failing to communicate honestly, and provides a structured typology of audiences and what each type of user needs.
Part III — Methods forms the core of the book. Eight distinct methods for communicating uncertainty are reviewed in depth:

title: Welcome
text:
How to Use This Book
This book can be read cover to cover, but it is also designed as a reference to be consulted as needed. Readers new to the topic should start with Part I before turning to the methods. Practitioners who already have a working understanding of uncertainty can proceed directly to Part III.
Each method chapter in Part III is self-contained and can be read independently.

title: Welcome
text:
About the Author
Bahman Rostami-Tabar Bahman is Professor of Analytics and Decision Sciences at Cardiff Business School, Cardiff University. He leads the Data Lab for Social Good, a research team at Cardiff Business School, Cardiff University. He also founded and is the Academic Director of the Art, Science and Uncertainty Summer School, an interdisciplinary summer school that takes place every year in Cardiff, Wales.
His research lies at the intersection of data, uncertainty & future, and decision-making, with a particular focus on how advanced analytical methods can support better decisions in complex and uncertain environments. Drawing on Data Science, Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Operations Research (OR), he develops and applies methods that are both methodologically rigorous and practically relevant to real-world decision problems.
He works closely with academic, public, and industry partners to address important societal challenges. His research covers uncertainty quantification and communication, explainable AI, predictive analytics, and decision sciences, with a particular focus on applications for social good, including healthcare operations, public and global health, supply chains, and sustainability.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.1 Structure of This Paper
Uncertainty is an inescapable feature of knowledge, decision-making, and communication across virtually every domain of human endeavour. A physician advising a patient on the likely progression of a disease, a meteorologist issuing a five-day forecast, an economist projecting next year’s growth, a hydrologist modelling flood risk, an intelligence analyst assessing the likelihood of a geopolitical event — all are working with incomplete, imperfect, or inherently variable information. In each case, how that uncertainty is understood, framed, and communicated to those who must act on it can have consequences as significant as the underlying knowledge itself.
Yet despite its ubiquity, uncertainty is frequently treated as an inconvenience to be minimised or hidden rather than a property of knowledge to be honestly represented. Point estimates are reported without ranges. Forecasts are presented as predictions. Probabilistic language is avoided for fear of appearing indecisive or undermining confidence. The result is a systematic mismatch between what is actually known and what is communicated — one that can mislead decision-makers, erode public trust, and contribute to poor outcomes. As Fischhoff & Davis (2014) argue, unless uncertainty is communicated effectively, decision makers may place too much or too little faith in the information they receive, with potentially serious consequences. The principle is stated simply by National Research Council (2006): no forecast is complete without a description of its uncertainty.
The challenge is compounded by a fundamental tension identified across the literature. Those who generate uncertain information — forecasters, scientists, analysts — frequently prefer to communicate it in vague, qualitative terms, partly because doing so reduces their accountability if a prediction turns out to be wrong (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). Those who consume uncertain information — the public, policymakers, patients, risk managers — typically prefer precise, numerical communication, because they need concrete boundaries to make high-stakes decisions (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). This sender-receiver mismatch is one of the most persistent and practically consequential problems in the field.
Communicating uncertainty is also genuinely difficult. Audiences differ in their statistical literacy, their tolerance for ambiguity, and their preferences for how information is presented. Methods that work well for expert audiences — such as probability distributions or quantile intervals — may confuse or alienate non-specialist readers. Simpler formats, such as categorical ratings or verbal expressions, risk oversimplifying or introducing their own forms of misunderstanding. There is no universally best method; what works depends on the message, the medium, and the audience. Furthermore, as Bles et al. (2019) demonstrate, uncertainty lacks a single agreed definition across disciplines, complicating both the production and reception of uncertainty communication at a fundamental level.
This paper aims to serve as a practical, cross-disciplinary reference for anyone who needs to communicate uncertainty — whether in a scientific report, a policy document, a data visualisation, a public health bulletin, or a financial forecast. It is written to be accessible to readers from any discipline, and draws on literature spanning statistics, meteorology, climate science, economics, medicine, psychology, intelligence analysis, ecology, and risk communication. The paper does not attempt to survey the full literature on uncertainty quantification, probabilistic modelling, or decision theory — each of these is a vast field in its own right. The focus is squarely on communication: how uncertain information is represented and conveyed to an audience, and how different representational choices affect understanding, trust, and decision-making.
The paper is organised as follows. Chapter 3 introduces uncertainty as a concept, reviewing key definitions and frameworks from across the literature, including the distinction between epistemic and aleatory uncertainty, Stirling’s four-dimensional matrix of incertitude, and the concepts of deep uncertainty and unknown unknowns. Chapter 4 examines where uncertainty comes from, identifying five major categories of source: measurement and data uncertainty, model uncertainty, parameter uncertainty, structural and scenario uncertainty, and human and judgmental uncertainty. Chapter 5 situates uncertainty among four closely related concepts — variability, volatility, randomness, and risk — clarifying how these differ and why the distinctions matter for communication. Chapter 6 asks why uncertainty should be communicated at all, examines the consequences of failing to do so, and considers the range of audiences who may need to engage with uncertain information, from technical experts to the general public. The core of the paper is Chapter 8, which reviews eight distinct approaches to communicating uncertainty: forecast trajectories, fan charts, probability distributions, prediction intervals and quantiles, categorical approaches, scenario and what-if analysis, verbal expressions, and numerical expressions. For each method, the paper discusses what it is, provides a practical example with a figure placeholder, examines its strengths and limitations, identifies when it is most appropriate, and offers good practice recommendations. Finally, Chapter 17 draws together cross-cutting principles, presents a summary table matching methods to audience types, and identifies open questions for future work.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.2 How to Use This Paper
This paper is designed to be read as a whole, but it can equally be used as a reference in which individual sections are consulted as needed. Readers who are new to the topic may wish to read Sections 3 through 6 before turning to the methods. Practitioners who already have a working understanding of uncertainty and are seeking guidance on communication methods may proceed directly to Chapter 8. Each method subsection in Chapter 8 is self-contained, with cross-references to the conceptual sections where relevant.






Note



A note on scope: This paper focuses on the communication of quantitative or semi-quantitative uncertainty — uncertainty that can be expressed, at least in part, through numbers, ranges, probabilities, or calibrated language. The communication of purely qualitative uncertainty, philosophical scepticism, and the sociological and political dimensions of uncertainty governance, while touched upon where relevant, are not the primary focus.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
text:
3.1 What is Uncertainty?
The word “uncertainty” is used freely across virtually every academic discipline, yet it carries subtly — and sometimes radically — different meanings depending on the field in which it appears. In philosophy it refers to the limits of knowledge; in physics it can mean the fundamental indeterminacy of quantum mechanics; in statistics it refers to the variability of an estimator; in economics it denotes the impossibility of assigning probabilities to outcomes; in everyday speech it simply means not knowing what will happen. As Makridakis & Bakas (2016) observe, uncertainty “lacks a unique definition and clear meaning that varies when used in different fields such as philosophy, physics, information science, economics, statistics, finance, politics, psychology, engineering and insurance.” This multiplicity of meanings is not merely terminological housekeeping — it has direct practical consequences. When a scientist reports that a finding is “uncertain,” a policymaker, a journalist, and a risk analyst may each understand something quite different by the statement. Conceptual clarity about what uncertainty means, and what it does not mean, is therefore a prerequisite for effective communication.
This section reviews the principal frameworks through which scholars have attempted to bring analytical order to the concept of uncertainty. It begins with the foundational distinction between epistemic and aleatory uncertainty, before broadening to consider ontological, linguistic, and deep uncertainty. It then examines Stirling’s influential matrix of incertitude and the related taxonomy of known and unknown unknowns. The section concludes with a discussion of the key disagreements in the literature and their implications for practice.
At its most general, uncertainty refers to a state of incomplete or imperfect knowledge — an inability to fully describe the present or reliably predict the future (Makridakis & Bakas, 2016). This everyday understanding, however, conceals important distinctions. A physician who cannot determine a diagnosis with confidence, a meteorologist issuing a five-day forecast, and an engineer assessing the structural integrity of a bridge under novel loading conditions are all dealing with uncertainty, yet the character of their uncertainty — its source, its reducibility, and the kind of information that would help resolve it — differs substantially in each case.
A critical philosophical distinction that underpins much of the subsequent literature is that uncertainty is fundamentally a property of knowledge, not of the world itself (Scoones, 2019). The future trajectory of a hurricane is not inherently uncertain; it is determined by physical laws. It appears uncertain to us because we lack the observational precision and computational power to fully model those laws. This epistemological framing — uncertainty as a gap in human knowledge rather than an objective feature of reality — has profound implications for how it should be communicated and managed. It suggests that uncertainty can, at least in principle, be reduced through better data, better models, and more careful reasoning, even if it can never be entirely eliminated.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
text:
3.2 Epistemic and Aleatory Uncertainty
The most widely used distinction in statistical and scientific practice is between epistemic and aleatory uncertainty, a division that maps roughly onto the contrast between ignorance and irreducible randomness.
Aleatory uncertainty is conceptualised as the inherent, irreducible stochastic variability within a system — the randomness that remains even with perfect knowledge of the underlying processes (Gruber et al., 2023). It represents, in Spiegelhalter (2017)’s formulation, the “inevitable unpredictability of the future due to unforeseeable factors, fully expressed by classical probabilities.” In financial contexts, aleatory uncertainty is closely related to what is termed latent volatility — the inherent fluctuation in asset prices that cannot be predicted regardless of how sophisticated the model (Eggen, 2025). The canonical example is the outcome of a fair coin toss: no amount of additional information about the coin or the person flipping it can, under normal circumstances, allow us to predict the result with certainty.
Epistemic uncertainty, by contrast, refers to a state of incomplete knowledge about the world that could, in theory, be resolved with more data or better models. It encompasses, as Spiegelhalter (2017) describes it, “uncertainty about the structure and parameters of statistical models, expressed, for example, through Bayesian probability distributions,” and is fundamentally reducible through improving models or data (Eggen, 2025). In the language of Bles et al. (2019), epistemic uncertainty concerns “the past and present state of the world that arises because of what we do not know but could know in theory.” A physician who is unsure of a diagnosis because a test result is not yet available faces epistemic uncertainty; the same physician uncertain about a patient’s idiosyncratic biological response to a treatment confronts something closer to aleatory uncertainty.
This distinction is practically significant because it implies different responses. Epistemic uncertainty motivates investment in better measurement, broader data collection, and more refined models. Aleatory uncertainty, by contrast, calls for honest acknowledgement of irreducible limits and for communication strategies that convey the range of possible outcomes rather than implying a determinism that does not exist.
However, the boundary between the two is less clean than it first appears. Gruber et al. (2023) argue forcefully that a simple decomposition into aleatory and epistemic uncertainty “does not do justice to the overarching problem of multiple sources of uncertainty.” Their central challenge is that the classification of uncertainty as aleatory or epistemic is entirely context-dependent, not a universal property of the phenomenon being studied. A dice roll, for instance, is conventionally treated as purely aleatory; yet if one possessed a model that perfectly measured the initial position, velocity, and rotation of the die, the outcome would become entirely predictable — effectively epistemic. As Gruber et al. (2023) conclude, “uncertainties can therefore only be distinguished on a model and data basis and not universally.” This insight is important for communication: claiming that a source of uncertainty is irreducibly aleatory may be more a statement about the limits of current models than about the nature of reality.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
text:
3.3 Ontological, Linguistic, and Deep Uncertainty
Beyond the epistemic-aleatory divide, scholars have identified further conceptual layers of uncertainty that resist standard probabilistic treatment.
Ontological uncertainty acknowledges the ultimate limits of mathematical modelling as a representation of a complex reality. Spiegelhalter (2017) defines it as “uncertainty about the entire modelling process as a description of reality” — the possibility that no model, however sophisticated, fully captures the dynamics of the system being studied. This form of uncertainty is particularly relevant in complex socio-technical or socio-ecological systems, where the interactions between human behaviour, institutional structures, and natural processes cannot be fully specified in advance (Scoones, 2019).
Linguistic uncertainty arises from the limits of human language in describing and communicating risk and uncertainty. It manifests as vagueness, where a term has imprecise boundaries; ambiguity, where a term has multiple possible meanings; and under-specificity, where a statement does not provide sufficient detail to constrain interpretation (Unknown Author, n.d.). Linguistic uncertainty is particularly prominent in qualitative risk assessments, where terms such as “low risk” or “possible” are routinely used without explicit reference to exposure conditions or probability scales, leaving audiences to fill in the gaps with their own prior beliefs.
Deep uncertainty defines a more radical condition in which the fundamental parameters of a problem cannot be agreed upon. Doyle et al. (2023) characterise it as a situation that “exists when experts or stakeholders do not know or cannot agree on conceptual models, uncertainty probability distributions, and how to weigh and value desirable alternative outcomes.” Deep uncertainty is not merely a quantitative problem — it is a problem of contested knowledge and competing value systems. Standard probabilistic tools are inadequate under conditions of deep uncertainty, and communication strategies must shift accordingly, from conveying precise probabilities to facilitating deliberation about alternative possible futures.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
section: 3.4 Stirling’s Matrix: The Four Dimensions of Incertitude
text:
3.4 Stirling’s Matrix: The Four Dimensions of Incertitude
One of the most influential conceptual frameworks for understanding the multidimensional character of uncertainty is the incertitude matrix proposed by Andy Stirling, which provides a more rigorous taxonomy than the catch-all term “uncertainty” alone can supply (Scoones, 2019; Stirling, n.d.b). The matrix is constructed on two axes: whether knowledge about outcomes (what could happen) is problematic or unproblematic, and whether knowledge about likelihoods (how probable each outcome is) is problematic or unproblematic. Crossing these two dimensions yields four distinct states of incertitude, each of which implies a different analytical and communicative response.
Risk occupies the quadrant where both outcomes and their probabilities can be reliably estimated (Scoones, 2019). This is the domain of classical probabilistic risk assessment — rolling dice, actuarial insurance tables, and well-studied engineering failure modes. In this condition, standard statistical tools are appropriate, and uncertainty can be quantified and communicated as a probability distribution or confidence interval.
Uncertainty, in the strict Knightian sense, describes conditions where possible outcomes are known but there is no reliable basis for assigning probabilities to them (Stirling, n.d.b). One might know that a new drug could be effective or ineffective, or that a policy intervention could succeed or fail, without having the empirical basis to attach meaningful probabilities to these possibilities. In this condition, probability estimates are formally illegitimate, and presenting them as though they were meaningful constitutes a “pretence of knowledge” (Stirling, n.d.a).
Ambiguity arises when probabilities might in principle be calculable, but there is fundamental disagreement about how to define or frame the possible outcomes (Scoones, 2019). This condition is common in contested policy domains — such as the risk of genetically modified organisms, or the long-term consequences of emerging technologies — where different scientific communities or stakeholder groups organise the problem space in incompatible ways.
Ignorance represents the most challenging condition: one in which neither the outcomes nor their probabilities can be fully characterised, and where parameters are “not only contestable but also — at least in part — unknown” (Stirling, n.d.a). This is the domain of “unknown unknowns” — genuinely unanticipated effects and emergent phenomena that fall entirely outside the existing analytical frame. Black Swan events, as described by Makridakis & Bakas (2016), are paradigmatic examples.






Figure placeholder — Stirling’s Incertitude Matrix
Suggested figure: A 2×2 matrix with axes labelled “Knowledge about outcomes” (unproblematic to problematic) and “Knowledge about likelihoods” (unproblematic to problematic), with the four quadrants labelled Risk, Ambiguity, Uncertainty, and Ignorance.
Source to use: Figure 1 in Scoones (2019) or Figure 3 in Stirling (n.d.b).
Action needed: Reproduce with permission or redraw based on the original.



A crucial epistemological point about this matrix, emphasised by both Stirling (n.d.b) and Scoones (2019), is that these four conditions are states of knowledge, not states of nature. They describe the relationship between an observer and a system, not objective properties of the system itself. The same phenomenon may be treated as a risk by a well-resourced modelling team with extensive historical data and as ignorance by a community with no access to such information. This relativity of uncertainty states has profound implications for governance and communication: it means that characterising a situation as “risky” rather than “uncertain” or “ambiguous” is always, to some degree, a social and political act, not merely a technical one.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
text:
3.5 Known and Unknown Unknowns
A complementary taxonomy that has gained wide currency in policy and forecasting contexts is the classification of uncertainty into four quadrants based on the interaction of what is known and unknown about both outcomes and mechanisms (Makridakis & Bakas, 2016). This framework, popularised through Donald Rumsfeld’s 2002 press conference but with deeper roots in decision theory, distinguishes between:

Known/Knowns: Situations where both outcomes and their likelihoods are understood. Standard statistical forecasting applies, uncertainty is measurable, and risk can be estimated within acceptable bounds.
Unknown/Knowns: Situations where relevant knowledge exists but is ignored, suppressed, or denied — often due to institutional pressures, cognitive biases, or wilful blindness. This is arguably the most dangerous category, as it involves knowable information being rendered operationally invisible.
Known/Unknowns: Situations where the forecaster is aware of their ignorance — they know they do not know. Uncertainty is large, often fat-tailed, and planning must proceed without reliable probability estimates.
Unknown/Unknowns: Genuinely unanticipated events that fall entirely outside the existing frame of analysis. These are Black Swan events that cannot be predicted by definition, but whose consequences can sometimes be moderated through robustness and adaptability strategies.

Makridakis et al. (2009) introduce a memorable distinction between two limiting cases of this taxonomy. Subway uncertainty represents the “known unknowns” that operate in controlled, well-understood environments — like the random delay of a subway train — where standard statistical models apply and uncertainty can be quantified. Coconut uncertainty, by contrast, encompasses the “unknown unknowns” characteristic of rare, catastrophic events — like a fatal accident caused by a falling coconut — where the event is “impossible to quantify or model… even though we can clearly expect these to occur.” This distinction highlights that not all uncertainty can be managed by the same tools, and that the aspiration to reduce all uncertainty to measurable risk is, in many domains, fundamentally misconceived.

Suggested figure: Table 1 in Makridakis & Bakas (2016), “Forecasting accuracy, uncertainty and risk: From Known/Knowns to Black Swans,” which maps Rumsfeld’s four quadrants against predictability, uncertainty type, and associated risk levels.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
section: 3.6 Disagreements and Tensions in the Literature
text:
3.6 Disagreements and Tensions in the Literature
Two significant tensions run through the conceptual literature on uncertainty and are worth making explicit.
The first concerns the epistemological status of the aleatory-epistemic distinction. While this division is widely taught in statistics and machine learning curricula, Gruber et al. (2023) argue that it is philosophically and mathematically untenable as a universal classification. Because whether a source of variation is treated as aleatory or epistemic depends on the model and data available rather than on any intrinsic property of the phenomenon, the distinction is always provisional. This challenges a common assumption in uncertainty communication — that some uncertainty is “irreducible” and must simply be reported rather than investigated. In reality, what counts as irreducible is always relative to the current state of knowledge.
The second concerns the institutional treatment of uncertainty. Stirling (n.d.a) documents a troubling pattern in which regulatory and environmental institutions, facing political pressure to produce clear and actionable numbers, routinely describe what is genuinely deep uncertainty as if it were calculable risk. The same word — “uncertainty” — is used to describe both a situation where probabilities are unknown and a situation where a specific probability has been confidently asserted. This institutional conflation, characterised as a “pretence of knowledge,” has the effect of foreclosing deliberation about alternative possibilities and concentrating decision-making authority in technical hands. For communicators, the practical implication is clear: the choice of how to frame uncertainty — as risk, as uncertainty, as ambiguity, or as ignorance — is never a purely technical decision, and its consequences deserve careful scrutiny.

title: 3  Conceptualising Uncertainty
text:
3.7 Good Practice

Resist reductionism: Practitioners should resist the temptation to close down complex uncertainty, ambiguity, or ignorance into simple probabilistic risk assessments. Applying risk-based mathematical approaches to deeply uncertain situations is, as Stirling (n.d.a) argues, “irrational, unscientific and potentially misleading.”
Address linguistic uncertainty: In qualitative assessments, communicators must actively minimise linguistic uncertainty by explicitly specifying the context in which probability terms are used, ensuring that exposure variables and reference classes are clearly defined (Unknown Author, n.d.).
Track the cascade of uncertainty: When developing models or assessments, analysts should explicitly categorise uncertainty across three dimensions — its location in the analytical pipeline, its nature (aleatory or epistemic), and its level (from determinism to deep ignorance) — to maintain a clear audit trail of assumptions (Pascoe et al., n.d.).
State clearly what type of uncertainty is being communicated: Before choosing a communication format, identify whether the uncertainty at hand is aleatory, epistemic, structural, or deep, as each type implies different appropriate formats and levels of precision, as detailed in Chapter 8.

title: 4  Sources of Uncertainty
text:
4.1 Measurement and Data Uncertainty
Understanding where uncertainty comes from is as important as understanding what it is. In practice, uncertainty does not emerge from a single point in the analytical process but cascades throughout the entire pipeline — from the initial collection of data, through the construction and calibration of models, to the interpretation and communication of results. As Bles et al. (2019) note, no consensus has emerged as to a general framework for categorising these sources, perhaps because the wide variety of contexts and tasks being considered makes any single taxonomy difficult to apply universally. For the purposes of this review, sources of uncertainty are grouped into five broad categories: measurement and data uncertainty, model uncertainty, parameter uncertainty, structural and scenario uncertainty, and human and judgmental uncertainty. These categories are not mutually exclusive — in practice, multiple sources interact and compound one another — but distinguishing them is essential for designing appropriate communication strategies.
Before choosing how to communicate uncertainty, communicators should ask a prior question: why does the uncertainty exist? Is it because of unavoidable natural variation, the difficulties of measurement, limited knowledge of underlying processes, or disagreement between experts? (Bles et al., 2019). The answer shapes both the appropriate method of communication and the degree of honesty required about the limits of what is known.
At the most foundational level, uncertainty is rooted in the physical and practical limits of observation, measurement, and data collection. No instrument is perfectly precise; no sampling procedure captures a population without error; no data set is complete. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change identifies data uncertainty as arising from “missing, inaccurate or non-representative data, inappropriate spatial or temporal resolution” (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, n.d.). In algorithmic and statistical forecasting, the specific concerns include omitted variables, measurement errors, and data that violate the assumptions of independence and identical distribution that underpin most standard models (Gruber et al., 2023).
Qualitative research with natural hazard scientists captures this source of uncertainty in vivid terms: a primary driver of uncertainty is simply “not having all the information or not having the ability to get the information,” leading to critical gaps in the empirical record that must either be filled with assumptions or acknowledged as unknowns (Doyle et al., 2023). The consequences of data gaps are often underappreciated precisely because they are invisible: by the time a dataset is analysed, its origins, incompleteness, and imperfections have typically been forgotten or omitted from the report.
Data uncertainty is also temporally dynamic rather than fixed. In macroeconomic statistics, for example, initial data estimates are subject to ongoing revision as statistical agencies succeed in incorporating additional information over subsequent months and years (Galvão et al., 2019). This means that the uncertainty associated with a GDP figure, for instance, is not a static quantity at the time of release but changes as the data vintage evolves — a dimension of uncertainty that is rarely communicated to users of economic statistics.

title: 4  Sources of Uncertainty
text:
4.2 Model Uncertainty
Even with perfect data, our models of the world are inevitably simplifications. Every model embeds assumptions about which variables matter, how they relate to one another, what functional forms govern those relationships, and how the system behaves under conditions that may differ from those in which the model was calibrated. As Mehta & Srivastava (2020) observe, “models are simplified representations of complex systems, and as such are never the ‘real’ thing.” Model uncertainty specifically encompasses the variation in outputs that occurs because “different models simulate different responses” to the same inputs — a phenomenon that is particularly well documented in climate science, where ensembles of competing models routinely produce divergent projections of future temperatures, precipitation, and sea levels (Mehta & Srivastava, 2020).
Practitioners are often acutely aware of model limitations, asking how accurate models are, what they can and cannot produce, and recognising that missing data inputs render the models themselves less effective (Doyle et al., 2023). Yet this awareness does not always translate into explicit communication to end users, who may receive model outputs presented as if they were direct observations of reality rather than outputs of a simplified and imperfect analytical apparatus.
A striking finding from the empirical forecasting literature, documented at length by Makridakis & Bakas (2016), is that more complex models do not reliably produce better predictions. Across a wide range of empirical studies, simple forecasting methods have matched or outperformed sophisticated statistical models in genuine out-of-sample tests. The reason is instructive: complex models are flexible enough to capture noise as well as signal during the estimation phase, but this very flexibility becomes a liability when conditions change — as they inevitably do. In communication terms, this implies that model complexity should not be conflated with model reliability, and that presenting a complex model’s output without acknowledging model uncertainty may convey a false sense of precision.

title: 4  Sources of Uncertainty
text:
4.3 Parameter Uncertainty
Closely related to model uncertainty is parameter uncertainty, which concerns the specific numerical values assigned to the variables within a model. Even when the structural form of a model is well specified, its parameters must be estimated from finite and imperfect data. This estimation process introduces uncertainty that propagates through all subsequent outputs.
Parameter uncertainty is perhaps the most commonly acknowledged form of uncertainty in quantitative science — it is the uncertainty captured by confidence intervals, standard errors, and credible intervals in Bayesian analyses. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (n.d.) identifies it as arising from “poorly known or changing model parameters” that govern the equations underpinning projections. In complex risk assessments, parameter uncertainty frequently manifests as “uncertainty about the values of parameters in scientific models,” which must be estimated or calibrated using imperfect historical data (Bles et al., 2019).
However, even this relatively well-understood form of uncertainty is systematically underestimated in practice. Makridakis & Bakas (2016) document that the confidence intervals produced by many standard forecasting methods are systematically too narrow — larger sample sizes do not always reduce forecast uncertainty as much as statistical theory suggests. This underestimation of parameter uncertainty is a direct source of overconfidence in communicated forecasts.

title: 4  Sources of Uncertainty
text:
4.4 Structural and Scenario Uncertainty
Structural uncertainty operates at a higher conceptual level than parameter uncertainty. It represents flaws or limitations in the fundamental theoretical architecture of a model — not in the values assigned to its parameters, but in the assumptions about which variables to include, how to define the boundaries of the system, and which processes and relationships to represent. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (n.d.) characterises structural uncertainty as arising from “inadequate models, incomplete or competing conceptual frameworks, lack of agreement on model structure, ambiguous system boundaries or definitions, or significant processes or relationships wrongly specified or not considered.”
Structural uncertainty is particularly insidious because it is the hardest to detect from within the modelling framework itself. A model that is structurally wrong in an important respect may still fit historical data reasonably well, because the fitting process can compensate for structural errors by assigning incorrect values to the parameters that are included. Only when the model is applied in a genuinely novel situation — one that lies outside the envelope of conditions in which it was estimated — do structural errors typically become apparent. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (n.d.) notes explicitly that “experts tend to underestimate structural uncertainty arising from incomplete understanding of or competing conceptual frameworks for relevant systems and processes,” leading to overconfident projections that fail to account for the possibility that the model is wrong in a fundamental way.
Closely related to structural uncertainty is scenario uncertainty, which captures the inherent unpredictability of human behaviour and social evolution. Because human choices — about energy use, political organisation, technological adoption, and economic behaviour — cannot be modelled deterministically, forecasting over sufficiently long horizons must account for divergent possible futures driven by different human decisions. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (n.d.) describes this as “projections of human behaviour not easily amenable to prediction (e.g. evolution of political systems).” In climate modelling, scenario uncertainty encompasses the full range of possible future emission trajectories as a function of hypothetical socioeconomic choices about demographic change and energy policy (Mehta & Srivastava, 2020). Unlike parameter or structural uncertainty, scenario uncertainty cannot in principle be reduced by collecting more data or building better models — it reflects the genuine openness of the future.

title: 4  Sources of Uncertainty
text:
4.5 Human and Judgmental Uncertainty
A final and often underappreciated source of uncertainty is introduced by the humans who collect, interpret, and communicate information. This source operates at multiple levels.
At the level of individual scientists, Doyle et al. (2023) document through qualitative interviews with natural hazard researchers that the “human element” is a profound driver of uncertainty. Scientists who prefer specific research designs or methodological approaches may inadvertently limit the scope of available evidence, “creating uncertainty” by the selectivity of what they choose to study. Beyond methodological preferences, uncertainty is generated by interpersonal dynamics: disagreements between researchers, ego-driven conflicts over interpretation, and varying standards of what constitutes rigorous evidence all introduce variability into the knowledge base that cannot be captured by formal uncertainty quantification (Doyle et al., 2023).
At the level of interpretation, it matters deeply “how data are interpreted, when, and by whom” (Doyle et al., 2023). The same dataset can yield different uncertainty assessments in the hands of different analysts, depending on their disciplinary training, their prior beliefs, and their institutional incentives. This interpretive layer of uncertainty is particularly consequential because it is rarely made transparent in published reports or public communications.
At the level of the broader communication ecosystem, Doyle et al. (2023) highlight that the media environment itself functions as an uncertainty generator: “conflicting advice is readily available online,” and the amplification of scientific disagreements through media coverage can produce a level of apparent uncertainty in the public mind that substantially exceeds the genuine epistemic uncertainty in the scientific community.
Finally, human uncertainty includes the systematic cognitive biases that cause forecasters and analysts to misrepresent their own uncertainty. Makridakis & Bakas (2016) document extensive evidence that judgmental forecasts are typically overconfident — that is, the stated confidence intervals are too narrow relative to the actual track record of the forecasts. The same overconfidence has been documented in medical diagnosis, where physicians rated their confidence only marginally lower for difficult cases than for easy ones, even when their diagnostic accuracy dropped from 55% to below 6% (Makridakis et al., 2009). This systematic miscalibration of human uncertainty is one of the strongest arguments for formal uncertainty quantification and explicit uncertainty communication, rather than relying on expert intuition alone.

title: 4  Sources of Uncertainty
text:
4.6 Good Practice

Use systematic typologies: To prevent critical gaps in uncertainty assessment, practitioners should actively “consider all plausible sources of uncertainty using a systematic typology of uncertainty” rather than relying on ad hoc identification of what seems most salient in a given context (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, n.d.).
Audit model assumptions: To address structural and parameter uncertainty, forecasters should “specify assumptions and system definitions clearly, compare models with observations for a range of conditions, and assess maturity of the underlying science” (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, n.d.). Model complexity should not be treated as a proxy for reliability.
Interrogate the source before choosing the format: Communicators should explicitly diagnose the source of uncertainty before engaging audiences. The question “why is there uncertainty?” — whether because of natural variation, measurement difficulties, limited process knowledge, or expert disagreement — should shape the choice of communication method and the level of precision that is appropriate (Bles et al., 2019).
Acknowledge the human element: Reports and communications should distinguish, where possible, between uncertainty that arises from the system being studied and uncertainty that arises from the choices and limitations of the analysts who studied it. Transparency about the human and institutional sources of uncertainty is itself a form of honest communication.

title: 5  Uncertainty and Related Concepts
text:
5.1 Uncertainty and Risk
The term “uncertainty” is frequently used as if it were interchangeable with a cluster of related concepts — risk, variability, randomness, and volatility — that in fact carry distinct technical meanings. This conflation is not merely an academic inconvenience; it has direct practical consequences for how information is communicated, how audiences interpret it, and how decisions are made. A communicator who presents variability data as if it were epistemic uncertainty, or who treats genuine uncertainty as if it were calculable risk, produces systematic distortions in audience understanding that no amount of presentational skill can fully correct. Conceptual clarity about the distinctions between these terms is therefore a prerequisite for effective communication.
As Makridakis & Bakas (2016) observe, “uncertainty” lacks a unique definition and is frequently treated as a linguistic catch-all, leading to theoretical confusion and flawed communication across disciplines. This section examines how uncertainty relates to and differs from four closely related concepts — risk, variability, randomness, and volatility — and concludes with a comparative summary table.
The distinction between uncertainty and risk is foundational to the field and originates in the classic economic framework established by Frank Knight in 1921. In the Knightian paradigm, risk denotes a state where all potential outcomes are known and their precise probabilities can be mathematically calculated (Scoones, 2019). The probability that a fair die shows a six is exactly one in six; the probability that a bridge collapses under a specified load can be estimated from engineering models and empirical data. Risk, in this strict sense, is measurable, and the tools of classical probability theory are fully appropriate.
Uncertainty, by contrast, defines a state where probabilities cannot be reliably assigned because the underlying mechanisms are unknown, the environment is genuinely novel, or the outcomes themselves are contested (Scoones, 2019). Frank Knight’s original argument was that profit in a market economy arises precisely from this kind of genuine uncertainty — from situations where no competitor can calculate the odds. Stirling (n.d.b) formalises this distinction within the incertitude matrix discussed in Section 3.4: risk occupies the quadrant where both outcomes and likelihoods are unproblematic, while uncertainty in the strict sense describes conditions where likelihoods cannot be confidently estimated even though the space of outcomes may be known.
Despite the clarity of this theoretical distinction, it is routinely violated in practice. Stirling (n.d.a) documents a troubling institutional pattern in which regulatory and environmental bodies, facing political pressure to produce clear and actionable numbers, use the word “uncertainty” to describe situations where they have in fact asserted a specific probability — treating deep uncertainty as if it were calculable risk. This conflation is characterised as an irresponsible “pretence of knowledge” that forecloses deliberation about alternative possibilities and concentrates decision-making authority in technical hands. For communicators, the implication is that the choice of framing — presenting something as a quantified risk versus an unquantifiable uncertainty — carries substantial political and epistemic weight and should not be made carelessly.

title: 5  Uncertainty and Related Concepts
text:
5.2 Uncertainty and Variability
Variability refers to the natural, objective heterogeneity that exists within a population or a system — the genuine differences between individuals, locations, or time points that reflect real physical, biological, or social diversity (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). Human body weights vary; regional rainfall varies; the time it takes different people to respond to a drug varies. Variability is a property of the world, not of our knowledge of it.
Epistemic uncertainty, by contrast, refers to the subjective limits of a forecaster’s or analyst’s knowledge regarding a specific, single true value. As Bles et al. (2019) put it, epistemic uncertainty concerns “the past and present state of the world that arises because of what we do not know but could know in theory.” When a scientist reports uncertainty about the average global temperature in a given year, they are reporting epistemic uncertainty about a single quantity, not variability in that quantity.
The practical importance of this distinction lies in how audiences interpret graphical displays of uncertainty. The literature consistently documents that non-expert audiences routinely conflate the two, misinterpreting statistical distributions or error bars designed to show a forecaster’s lack of knowledge — epistemic uncertainty — as maps of physical population variation — variability (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). A patient shown a probability distribution over possible treatment outcomes may interpret it as showing how different patients respond, rather than how uncertain the physician is about this specific patient’s likely response. This misinterpretation can lead to systematically distorted risk perceptions and poorly calibrated decisions.
The communication implication is direct: whenever a display is intended to convey epistemic uncertainty about a single quantity, the accompanying text must explicitly clarify that the distribution represents a state of knowledge, not a description of population heterogeneity. As European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019) recommend, communicators should “explain clearly that the distribution represents uncertainty about the quantity of interest, for which there is a single true value.”

title: 5  Uncertainty and Related Concepts
text:
5.3 Uncertainty and Randomness
Randomness — what statisticians and machine learning researchers call aleatory uncertainty — describes the stochastic behaviour of a system that generates different outcomes even when initial conditions appear identical (Spiegelhalter, 2017). It is the irreducible variability of a chaotic or probabilistic process: the unpredictable fluctuations of a Brownian particle, the decay time of a radioactive atom, the outcome of a fair coin toss. Randomness is characterised by the fact that collecting more data or building a better model cannot, in principle, eliminate it — it reflects an inherent property of the system, not a limitation of the observer (Gruber et al., 2023).
Epistemic uncertainty, in contrast, represents a reducible gap in human knowledge: the uncertainty that arises because we do not know what the true value of a parameter is, which of several competing models is correct, or how a system will respond under novel conditions. In principle, additional information could resolve epistemic uncertainty; no additional information can resolve randomness.
This distinction has important practical implications. Communicating a source of uncertainty as “random” implies that it is irreducible and that further investment in data or modelling will not help. Communicating the same source as epistemic implies that it could, in principle, be reduced with more resources or better methods. As Gruber et al. (2023) point out, however, the boundary between randomness and epistemic uncertainty is not always as clear as textbook presentations suggest. A dice roll is conventionally treated as purely random; yet if one possessed a sufficiently detailed physical model of the dice, the throw, and the surface, the outcome would become perfectly predictable. The classification of uncertainty as aleatory or epistemic is therefore always relative to the current state of knowledge and the available models — not an absolute property of the phenomenon.

title: 5  Uncertainty and Related Concepts
text:
5.4 Uncertainty and Volatility
Volatility is the specific manifestation of aleatory uncertainty — randomness — within financial and economic time series. It refers to the degree of variance and the rapidity of fluctuations in market prices, exchange rates, or economic indicators over time (Eggen, 2025). Volatility is, in this sense, measurable: statistical models such as GARCH (Generalised Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity) exist precisely to estimate and forecast the conditional variance of financial returns, and market-implied volatility indices such as the VIX provide real-time quantitative measures of expected price variability (Makridakis & Bakas, 2016).
Volatility is therefore distinct from uncertainty in the strict Knightian sense. A highly volatile financial asset — one whose price fluctuates dramatically from day to day — may nonetheless operate within a risk framework if the statistical distribution of those fluctuations is well characterised and stable. The uncertainty that cannot be captured by standard volatility models is of a different character: it arises during structural breaks, crises, and Black Swan events, where the distributional assumptions that underpin the model are violated and the parameters of the volatility process itself change in ways that were not anticipated (Scoones, 2019). In such conditions, volatility modelling breaks down and the system moves from the domain of measurable risk into the domain of genuine uncertainty or ignorance.
Scoones (2019)’s discussion of the 2007–08 financial crisis illustrates this distinction vividly. Pre-crisis, the banking system operated in a world of apparent risk: volatility was being measured, hedged, and traded. The crisis revealed that beneath this veneer of quantified risk lay deep uncertainty about the structure of network dependencies, the reliability of rating agency assessments, and the behaviour of interconnected institutions under stress — uncertainties that no volatility model had captured or could have captured.

title: 5  Uncertainty and Related Concepts
text:
5.6 Disagreements in the Literature
Two disagreements are worth noting. The first concerns the institutional conflation of risk and uncertainty described above: Stirling (n.d.a) argues this is an active political choice with serious governance implications, while mainstream policy and regulatory practice treats it as a pragmatic necessity. The second concerns the status of the aleatory-epistemic boundary: Gruber et al. (2023) challenge the assumption that randomness and epistemic uncertainty are cleanly separable categories, arguing that the boundary is model-dependent and therefore always provisional. Both disagreements have practical consequences for communication: if the boundary between uncertainty and risk is political rather than technical, then choosing how to frame a situation has ethical dimensions that communicators should acknowledge; and if the boundary between randomness and epistemic uncertainty is provisional, then claiming that a source of uncertainty is irreducible should be treated with appropriate caution.

title: 5  Uncertainty and Related Concepts
text:
5.7 Good Practice

Explicitly distinguish uncertainty from variability in text: Because audiences routinely interpret distributional graphics as showing population variability rather than epistemic uncertainty, communicators must always provide explicit textual clarification that the display represents a state of knowledge about a single quantity (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Use subjective terminology for epistemic uncertainty: To prevent audiences from confusing expert epistemic judgements with population frequencies, use the term “percentage certainty” (e.g., “experts are 90% certain”) rather than “percentage probability” when describing subjective assessments, since laypeople associate “probability” with the physical frequency of random events (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Do not present deep uncertainty as risk: When probabilities cannot be reliably assigned, communicators should resist the institutional pressure to produce a number. Presenting deep uncertainty as if it were calculable risk is misleading and can be “irrational, unscientific and potentially misleading” (Stirling, n.d.a).
Acknowledge when volatility modelling reaches its limits: In financial and economic communication, be explicit about the conditions under which standard volatility models remain valid and the conditions — structural breaks, crises, genuinely novel events — under which they do not.

title: 6  Why Communicate Uncertainty, and to Whom?
text:
6.1 The Case for Communicating Uncertainty Honestly
The question of why uncertainty should be communicated at all might seem to answer itself: honesty demands it, and good decision-making requires it. Yet in practice, the communication of uncertainty is far from routine. Forecasters suppress ranges to appear authoritative. Institutions present contested estimates as settled facts. Scientists avoid probabilistic language for fear of being misunderstood. Physicians project confidence they do not fully possess. Understanding why these tendencies are so pervasive — and why resisting them matters — requires engaging with both the positive case for uncertainty communication and the documented consequences of its absence. This section makes that case, then turns to the equally important question of to whom uncertainty should be communicated, and how the needs of different audiences shape the appropriate approach.
The fundamental argument for communicating uncertainty rests on the premise that “all prediction is inherently uncertain and effective communication of uncertainty information benefits users’ decisions” (National Research Council, 2006). Because uncertainty is an inescapable reality of complex systems, no forecast is complete without a description of its uncertainty (National Research Council, 2006). To present a forecast, projection, or scientific estimate without an honest account of its uncertainty is to misrepresent the state of knowledge — and to deprive decision-makers of information they need to act wisely.
Honest uncertainty communication serves several distinct and mutually reinforcing purposes. The first is calibration: if scientists fail to articulate the boundaries of their knowledge, audiences cannot appropriately weigh the evidence. As Fischhoff & Davis (2014) argue, “unless that uncertainty is communicated effectively, decision makers may put too much or too little faith in it.” Over-reliance on a deterministic forecast that subsequently fails can be more damaging than the underlying event itself, because it destroys the institutional credibility that is necessary for future communications to be trusted. Conversely, communicating genuine uncertainty allows users to make plans that are robust across a range of possible outcomes, rather than brittle strategies optimised for a single predicted scenario.
The second purpose is institutional transparency. Qualitative research with natural hazard scientists reveals that audiences respond positively to honest acknowledgement of limits: “it always helps if they’re open and honest, just share that they don’t quite know” (Doyle et al., 2023). This transparency builds rather than erodes trust — a finding that directly contradicts the common assumption among communicators that expressing uncertainty will undermine their credibility. When institutional actors suppress uncertainty to appear authoritative, they may achieve short-term confidence at the cost of long-term trust, particularly when events reveal that the suppressed uncertainty was real.
The third purpose concerns decision quality. Effective risk management “depends on information about the full range of possible consequences and associated probabilities rather than brittle, deterministic planning” (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2015). A decision-maker who knows only the most likely outcome of an intervention is poorly equipped to plan for the possibility that the outcome falls elsewhere. A decision-maker who also knows the range of plausible outcomes and their relative probabilities can build contingency plans, hedge against adverse possibilities, and calibrate the level of precaution to the actual level of uncertainty. Uncertainty communication, in this light, is not an admission of weakness but a prerequisite for sophisticated and adaptive decision-making.
The fourth purpose is scientific progress. Doyle et al. (2023) note that openly admitting data limitations and analytical uncertainties “encourages other scientists to say ‘oh well, let’s have a look at it’ — and that actually improves the science.” Scientific knowledge advances through the identification and investigation of gaps; suppressing uncertainty conceals those gaps and removes the incentive to fill them.

title: 6  Why Communicate Uncertainty, and to Whom?
section: 6.2 Consequences of Failing to Communicate Uncertainty
text:
6.2 Consequences of Failing to Communicate Uncertainty
The consequences of failing to communicate uncertainty honestly are well documented across multiple domains. In medicine, overconfident diagnostic communication — where physicians present diagnoses with greater certainty than the evidence warrants — leads to reduced uptake of second opinions, delays in identifying errors, and unnecessary harm to patients. Studies show that physicians rated their confidence as only marginally lower for difficult diagnostic cases than for easy ones, even when their accuracy dropped from 55% to below 6% (Makridakis & Bakas, 2016). A physician who is 64% confident in a diagnosis that is correct less than 6% of the time is unlikely to seek confirmatory evidence or disclose uncertainty to the patient — yet both actions are precisely what would be appropriate.
In finance, the suppression of genuine uncertainty beneath a veneer of quantified risk contributed directly to the 2007–08 financial crisis. The belief that complex derivative instruments had been fully de-risked through mathematical modelling created systemic overconfidence that left institutions unprepared for the structural failure that followed (Scoones, 2019). In climate policy, the failure to communicate the full range of uncertainty in long-term projections has contributed to both underreaction — when people treat the lower bound of a range as the likely outcome — and overreaction — when single extreme scenarios dominate public discourse without appropriate probabilistic context.
Failing to communicate uncertainty also removes agency from those who would benefit from it most. Doyle et al. (2023) argue that honest communication of uncertainty “gives the public more agency to make decisions” in relation to natural hazards and risks. When institutional communicators withhold uncertainty — even with protective intent — they implicitly decide that the public cannot handle complex information. The evidence reviewed in this paper suggests that this assumption is empirically unwarranted: ordinary people can understand and use probabilistic information, and they make better decisions when they have it (Raftery, 2016).

title: 7  Audiences and Their Needs
text:
7.0.1 Entry Level: General Public, Patients, and General Media
Not all users of uncertain information need the same thing. One of the most important and consistently supported findings in the uncertainty communication literature is that different audiences have fundamentally different goals, different levels of statistical literacy, and different tolerances for complexity when engaging with uncertain information (National Research Council, 2006). A format that serves an expert statistician may actively mislead a general practitioner; a simplification appropriate for a newspaper headline may be insufficient for a policy analyst who needs to set regulatory thresholds. Designing uncertainty communication without first identifying the audience and their specific decision context is a recipe for poor outcomes regardless of the technical quality of the underlying analysis.
The most structured audience taxonomy in the reviewed literature is that developed by European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019), which divides audiences into three tiers based on their familiarity with scientific assessment, their knowledge of institutional processes, and the nature of the decision they need to make. This taxonomy provides a practical organising framework for matching communication methods to audiences, and its three levels — Entry, Informed, and Technical — are used throughout Chapter 8 to guide the “When to Use” recommendations for each communication approach.
The entry-level audience encompasses individuals with “little or no familiarity with scientific assessment” and “little or no knowledge of institutional processes and relationships” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). This group includes members of the general public engaging with public health communications, patients making decisions about medical treatments, and consumers of mainstream news media covering scientific topics. Their engagement with uncertain information is typically brief, emotionally charged, and embedded in a specific decision context — whether to evacuate before a storm, whether to accept a medical intervention, whether to change behaviour in response to a public health recommendation.
For this audience, the communication goal is often simple behavioural signalling rather than full probabilistic comprehension. Spiegelhalter (2017) recommends providing a “top gist” — a clear, memorable summary of the most decision-relevant aspect of the uncertain information — before layering any additional detail. The evidence reviewed by Bles et al. (2020) suggests that communicating uncertainty to this audience using simple numerical ranges alongside a central estimate is the most robust approach: it does not substantially decrease trust in the source, and it avoids the ambiguity problems associated with verbal expressions. Complex distributional displays, fan charts, and full probability density functions are generally inappropriate for this audience and should be avoided.

title: 7  Audiences and Their Needs
text:
7.1 Disagreements in the Literature
Two significant tensions run through the literature on why and to whom uncertainty should be communicated, and both have direct implications for practice.
The first is what might be called the uncertainty paradox. On one hand, robust experimental evidence from Bles et al. (2020) demonstrates that the provision of numerical uncertainty — particularly as a numeric range — does not substantially decrease public trust in either the numbers or the source. This is reassuring for communicators who worry that expressing uncertainty will undermine their credibility. On the other hand, Leffrang & Müller (2025) find that highly effective uncertainty visualisations — specifically ensemble spaghetti plots that accurately depict extreme volatility — can cause individuals to become “alienated and less likely to take the advice,” actively reducing their trust in algorithmic guidance. The apparent contradiction between these findings suggests that the relationship between uncertainty communication and trust is not linear: modest, well-calibrated uncertainty communication preserves or even enhances trust, while overwhelming visualisations of volatility and extreme scenarios may cross a threshold beyond which the audience disengages. The implication for practice is that communicators should aim for appropriate honesty about uncertainty, not maximal transparency at any cost.
The second tension concerns the persistent conflict between sender and receiver preferences. Dhami & Mandel (2022) document that senders — the scientists, forecasters, and analysts who generate uncertain information — strongly prefer to express that uncertainty in vague, verbal terms, because doing so provides “face-saving” flexibility and reduces their accountability if a prediction turns out to be wrong. Receivers — the policymakers, public, and patients who must act on the information — strongly prefer precise, numerical formats, because they need concrete boundaries to make consequential decisions. This mismatch is not merely a matter of different tastes; it has direct consequences for decision quality. When communicators default to vague verbal expressions for self-protective reasons, they systematically deprive their audiences of the information those audiences need most. Dhami & Mandel (2022) conclude that “when it really matters, both senders and receivers prefer the precise numeric expression of uncertainty” — a finding that suggests the sender’s preference for vagueness may itself reflect a form of motivated reasoning rather than a genuine assessment of what serves the audience.

title: 7  Audiences and Their Needs
text:
7.2 Good Practice

Determine decision relevance first: Before designing an uncertainty communication, identify the specific decision the audience is trying to make. Fischhoff & Davis (2014) recommend assessing whether the audience is “looking for a signal, choosing among fixed options, or learning to create options,” and tailoring the depth and format of the uncertainty information to that specific decision context.
Segment the audience: Never assume a monolithic audience. Practitioners must segment audiences into target groups and identify their needs, beliefs, and statistical skills before developing communication materials (Spiegelhalter, 2017). Different groups within the same communication context — for example, specialist journalists and general readers of the same news outlet — may require fundamentally different treatments of the same uncertainty information.
Layer the information: To satisfy multiple audiences simultaneously, employ a tiered or layered communication approach. Provide a clear top-line gist for entry-level audiences, followed by progressively more complex layers — numerical intervals, source explanations, and technical appendices — for informed and technical users (Spiegelhalter, 2017).
Develop, test, and evaluate: Communication products should not be released on the assumption that they will be understood as intended. Spiegelhalter (2017) recommend developing materials with target groups, testing them for comprehension, and evaluating their effects on decision-making before wide deployment.
Resist the face-saving temptation: Communicators should resist the institutional and personal incentives to suppress or vague-ify uncertainty for reasons of self-protection. The evidence reviewed in this section suggests that honest, calibrated uncertainty communication preserves institutional trust over the long run, while suppression of uncertainty creates fragility that is revealed when events diverge from deterministic predictions.

title: 8  Methods for Communicating Uncertainty
text:
The eight methods reviewed in this section represent the principal approaches through which uncertain information is represented and conveyed to audiences across disciplines. They range from fully visual methods — forecast trajectories, fan charts, and distributional displays — through numerical summaries — prediction intervals and quantiles — to categorical systems, narrative approaches, and linguistic and numerical expressions of probability. No single method is universally superior; each has particular strengths, characteristic limitations, and audiences for which it is best suited. The review of each method follows a consistent structure: an opening description of what the method is and how it works; a practical example with a figure placeholder indicating a specific source figure to reproduce or recreate; an assessment of strengths and limitations, including where the literature is divided; guidance on when the method is most appropriate; and a set of evidence-based good practice recommendations.
A cross-cutting theme across all eight methods is the importance of calibration — the property that stated probabilities correspond to observed frequencies. A method that is technically sophisticated but poorly calibrated provides a false picture of uncertainty and may harm decision-making more than a simpler but well-calibrated alternative. Calibration should be verified and reported wherever possible, regardless of the method chosen.
A second cross-cutting theme is the tension between completeness and cognitive load. More informative displays — full distributions, ensemble trajectories, multi-band fan charts — communicate more about the structure of uncertainty but impose greater demands on the audience. Simpler displays — point estimates, categorical labels, verbal expressions — reduce cognitive burden but at the cost of information. Matching the level of complexity to the audience and the decision context, as discussed in Chapter 6, is the central design challenge across all methods.

title: 9  Forecast Trajectories
text:
Forecast trajectories — frequently referred to in the literature as ensemble plots or spaghetti plots — are a visual method for communicating uncertainty by plotting multiple predicted future paths of a variable simultaneously on a single graph (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). Each individual line represents one possible future, generated by varying the input parameters of a forecasting model or by combining outputs from different models. Rather than collapsing the full range of possible futures into a single summary statistic or a smoothed probability band, forecast trajectories present the raw plurality of predictions, allowing the viewer to see both the central tendency and the diversity of possible outcomes simultaneously. The theoretical foundation of this approach is rooted in the frequency format hypothesis, which frames uncertainty as a set of discrete, countable alternative realities rather than as an abstract probability distribution (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). By making individual possible futures visible, the method aims to convey the genuine openness of the future in a way that more abstract representations cannot.
Beyond simply displaying multiple lines, trajectory forecasts are technically distinguished by their ability to capture the correlation structure between successive time steps within a forecasting horizon. Unlike marginal, point-in-time distributions — which describe the range of possible values at each future moment in isolation — trajectory forecasts model the full “path-dependency” of a sequence of outcomes, capturing how earlier values constrain and inform later ones (Kley-Holsteg & Ziel, 2020). Methods such as Gaussian copulas are used to preserve this dependency structure, ensuring that “not only the mean and the marginal properties, but also the correlation structure between hours within the forecasting horizon” are faithfully represented (Kley-Holsteg & Ziel, 2020). This distinction is consequential: for any decision that depends on the cumulative or aggregate behaviour of a variable over time — rather than its value at a single future moment — only a trajectory forecast that preserves the temporal correlation structure can provide reliable probabilistic guidance (Gilbert et al., 2021).

9.0.1 Practical Example
Forecast trajectories have been applied across a wide range of forecasting domains. In meteorology, they are used to visualise the potential tracks of tropical cyclones, with individual paths representing outputs from distinct weather models (Spiegelhalter, 2017). In hydrology, hydrologists use these plots to display river flow over time, tracing the individual predictions of different ensemble members to communicate the uncertainty in flood forecasts (Pappenberger et al., 2013). In public health, ensemble trajectories have been applied to epidemiological forecasting, including the prediction of COVID-19 hospitalisation numbers during the pandemic (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). In demography, population forecasters use trajectories to visualise randomly simulated future fertility rates, allowing users to see historical variability projected forward (Raftery & Ševčíková, 2023).
Three further applications illustrate the practical value of the correlation- preserving properties of trajectory forecasts. In water supply management, Kley-Holsteg & Ziel (2020) use probabilistic multi-step-ahead trajectory forecasts to simulate the aggregate path of demand over a 24-hour horizon, enabling planners to assess “the probability with which a water storage capacity can guarantee the supply over a certain period” — a question that cannot be answered by marginal probability intervals alone. In offshore wind energy operations, Gilbert et al. (2021) use sea-state trajectory forecasts to predict “weather windows” for safely transferring technicians to turbines: because the transfer window requires safe vessel conditions continuously throughout the entire pick-up and return journey, a marginal forecast of conditions at a single time point is insufficient, and only a trajectory forecast can determine whether conditions will remain safe throughout the full operational window. In intraday electricity trading, Serafin et al. (2022) use trajectory forecasts of electricity prices to construct “simultaneous prediction bands” over consecutive trading windows, enabling algorithmic strategies to identify optimal moments to sell renewable energy by detecting when the probability of a price path exceeding a target threshold is sufficiently high.

This figure provides an excellent pedagogical illustration of why modelling the correlation structure of trajectories matters for cumulative decisions.
