9.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Forecast trajectories offer several distinctive advantages over summary-based displays. First, individual trajectories capture the true, noisy evolution of a system over time. As Raftery & Ševčíková (2023) note, averaging or smoothing trajectories suppresses the stochastic variation that is a genuine feature of many real-world processes, and a smooth summary trajectory “would be very unlikely” to actually occur — it represents a statistical artefact rather than a plausible future. By contrast, individual trajectories convey the jerky, discontinuous character of actual realisations in a way that interval-based displays cannot.
Second, by explicitly illustrating alternative futures, ensemble trajectories intuitively convey that there is no guaranteed outcome, thereby preventing deterministic anchoring — the tendency of audiences to treat a central estimate as a definitive prediction (Toet et al., 2016). The visual presence of multiple lines signals directly that uncertainty is real and that the future is genuinely open.
Third, visualising discrete ensemble members can raise users’ awareness of severe, unlikely events that might be obscured by summary statistics (Ripberger et al., 2022). A tail trajectory that diverges dramatically from the central cluster makes the possibility of an extreme outcome viscerally present in a way that an interval boundary cannot.
Fourth, for professional users, the visual density of multiple lines implies that a rich web of modelling information has gone into the forecast, which can build confidence in the robustness of the underlying analysis (Pappenberger et al., 2013).
Fifth, and most distinctively for decision contexts that involve cumulative or time-dependent outcomes, trajectory forecasts provide information that no marginal distribution or prediction interval can supply. Kley-Holsteg & Ziel (2020) demonstrate that trajectory forecasts “provide additional information about the expected aggregated or cumulative” outcome over a planning horizon, and Serafin et al. (2022) show that simultaneous prediction bands — which guarantee that a stated percentage of complete price paths lie within specified boundaries over the full horizon — can only be constructed from joint trajectory distributions, not from point-in-time intervals.
Limitations. Against these strengths must be set several well-documented limitations. The most fundamental is visual clutter and cognitive overload: displaying too many trajectories can result in a visual chaos that impairs accurate interpretation, an effect so common that it has given the method its colloquial name (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). This complexity frequently overwhelms general audiences; qualitative studies have documented responses such as “I have no clue which one to believe” when members of the public are shown hurricane ensemble plots (Toet et al., 2016). Gilbert et al. (2021) confirm that raw trajectory visualisations produce “uncertainty information [that] is difficult to interpret by any decision maker” in operational high-stakes scheduling contexts.
A second limitation concerns misdirected attention. While trajectories highlight unlikely alternative futures, they may simultaneously draw attention away from the specific scenarios that forecasters consider most probable (Ripberger et al., 2022). The equal visual weight given to all ensemble members, regardless of their probability, can create a misleading impression of uniform likelihood across the displayed range.
Third, despite the probabilistic intent of ensemble plots, users frequently subvert it by selecting a single trajectory to follow as if it were a deterministic forecast (Pappenberger et al., 2013). This “cherry-picking” defeats the purpose of the ensemble representation.
Fourth, Leffrang & Müller (2025) identify an uncertainty paradox: when uncertainty visualisations are highly effective at depicting extreme volatility, audiences may become alienated and less likely to act on the guidance being communicated.
Fifth, and critically for technical practitioners, traditional point-wise evaluation metrics — including the Mean Absolute Error, Root Mean Squared Error, and the Pinball score — are entirely “blind” to the temporal correlation structure of trajectory forecasts (Kley-Holsteg & Ziel, 2020). They cannot detect whether a simulated path is structurally realistic or has been manipulated to have the correct marginal properties at each time step while exhibiting entirely unrealistic path behaviour. This means that practitioners who evaluate trajectory forecasts using standard metrics may be unknowingly deploying poorly calibrated path forecasts.
Disagreement in the literature. The literature is actively divided on whether ensemble plots ultimately help or harm lay decision-makers. Some researchers argue that they successfully align risk perceptions with actual risk (Leffrang & Müller, 2025), while others find that they confuse users and degrade trust relative to simpler prediction intervals (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). Among practitioners, there is a fundamental disagreement about the value of displaying individual paths: some value the ability to track individual ensemble members, while others argue that such plots are displayed only because they “allow misuse of probabilistic forecasts by following a single line” (Pappenberger et al., 2013).


9.0.3 When to Use This Method
Forecast trajectories are most appropriate when the communication goal requires illustrating the inherent volatility and stochastic variation of a system — particularly when summary intervals would falsely imply a smooth, linear progression inconsistent with the actual character of the process (Raftery & Ševčíková, 2023). They are essential — and irreplaceable by marginal methods — when the decision depends on the cumulative or aggregate behaviour of a variable over multiple time steps, or on whether conditions remain within specified bounds throughout an extended window (Gilbert et al., 2021; Kley-Holsteg & Ziel, 2020).
They are most suitable for technical audiences who are familiar with ensemble methods. For entry-level and informed audiences, they should be used with caution and only after extensive cognitive testing. They are least appropriate when the primary communication goal is a simple behavioural signal or a single actionable threshold — in such cases, the transformation of raw trajectories into categorical probability summaries, as described in the good practice recommendations below, is strongly preferable.


9.0.4 Good Practice

Limit the number of trajectories: Restrict the number of plotted lines to prevent visual overload. An excessive number of ensemble members does not enhance decision-making and actively undermines trust (Leffrang & Müller, 2025).
Use sequential presentation: Introduce stochasticity gradually — first a small number of individual trajectories, then summary distributions — to help audiences build intuitive understanding before confronting the full ensemble (Raftery & Ševčíková, 2023).
Transform raw trajectories into categorical risk summaries: Rather than showing end-users raw trajectory lines, transform the ensemble into operational risk categories (e.g., “Good, Fair, Poor, No-Go”) and visualise the percentage of trajectories in each category at each time step (Gilbert et al., 2021). This distillation into actionable probabilities preserves the information content of the full trajectory forecast while reducing cognitive demand to a level manageable for operational decision-makers.
Distinguish probability from visual weight: Make clear that equal visual weight does not imply equal probability. Use visual encoding — line thickness, colour saturation — to convey relative likelihood where trajectories differ in probability.
Accompany with a central estimate: Always provide a clearly labelled central estimate or median trajectory alongside the ensemble as a reference point.
Use the Energy Score for evaluation: When evaluating whether a trajectory forecast has captured the correct temporal correlation structure, use strictly proper multivariate scoring rules such as the Energy Score or the Variogram Score rather than standard point-wise metrics such as MAE or RMSE, which are entirely insensitive to path dependency (Gilbert et al., 2021; Kley-Holsteg & Ziel, 2020; Serafin et al., 2022).
Test with the intended audience: Given the documented confusion effects in non-expert populations, always conduct cognitive testing of ensemble displays with the target audience before deployment.

title: 10  Fan Charts
text:
Fan charts are visual displays of probabilistic forecasts over time that represent the full range of uncertainty as a series of nested, shaded bands radiating outward from a central estimate — producing the characteristic widening shape from which the method takes its name. The defining visual feature of a fan chart is that “the range of uncertainty inherent in the forecasts broadens with the forecast horizon” (Bank of England, n.d.), an insight that the graphic encodes directly and intuitively: the further into the future the forecast extends, the wider the fan becomes. Typically, the central band represents the modal — most likely — forecast, while surrounding regions of progressively lighter shading or different colours encode decreasing probability bands, representing “the range of possible outcomes and the subjective probabilities that the actual outcomes will fall within each range” (Bank of England, n.d.). As Bles et al. (2019) describe them, fan charts display “the bounds of several different confidence intervals, often coloured to emphasise the changing probability density going further from the point estimate.” Unlike spaghetti plots, which show individual trajectories, fan charts present a smoothed, continuous summary of the full probability distribution across all forecast horizons simultaneously, making the structure of uncertainty immediately visible without requiring the viewer to integrate over multiple individual lines.

10.0.1 Practical Example
The most prominent and widely studied application of fan charts is in macroeconomic forecasting. The Bank of England’s Monetary Policy Committee pioneered the use of fan charts in 1997 to communicate probabilistic projections of inflation and GDP growth to policymakers, financial markets, and the public (Spiegelhalter et al., 2011). In these charts, the central interval represents 10% probability, and the largest interval 90% probability, calculated using a frequency interpretation of historical forecast errors (Spiegelhalter et al., 2011). The Bank’s fan charts have since become an international reference point for uncertainty communication in economic policy, and have been adapted by the UK Office for National Statistics to visualise both historical and forecasted uncertainties surrounding GDP growth (Galvão et al., 2019).

10.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Fan charts offer a number of genuine communicative advantages. For audiences with statistical training, they are highly informative, providing “the most effective” means of conveying uncertainty to “statisticians and other expert users with familiarity of probability distribution functions” (Galvão et al., 2019). The widening shape of the graphic acts as a powerful visual metaphor that communicates the structure of forecasting uncertainty intuitively and without requiring any statistical interpretation: the further out the forecast, the less certain it becomes. Bank of England (n.d.) argue that this makes fan charts an effective vehicle for conveying “the high degree of uncertainty in economic forecasts, especially at longer horizons, an important message for the public to hear.”
Fan charts also have a distinctive advantage in their ability to communicate the balance of risks around a central forecast. Because the shading can be applied asymmetrically, a “lopsided” fan chart visually communicates whether forecasters place more weight on outcomes falling above or below the central projection (Bank of England, n.d.). This depiction of distributional skewness — that the risks are tilted to the upside or the downside — is difficult to convey through intervals or point estimates alone, and is of direct relevance to policy makers and financial market participants who need to understand whether the risks around a forecast are symmetric or directional.
Limitations. Against these strengths must be set several well-documented limitations. The most significant is that the information density of fan charts “may be too complicated for many users” (Galvão et al., 2019). In expert surveys, professionals who were themselves comfortable with fan charts acknowledged concerns that “many journalists would not understand it and certainly would not pass it on to the public” (Galvão et al., 2019). For entry-level audiences, the simultaneous presentation of multiple nested probability bands, each requiring the viewer to hold in mind a different probability level, imposes a cognitive load that substantially exceeds what non-specialist users can typically manage.
A second limitation is the persistence of the deterministic construal error even in the presence of explicitly displayed uncertainty bands. Despite the visual prominence of the fan, end-users frequently ignore the uncertainty information entirely; as Galvão et al. (2019) observe, “much of the subsequent discussion” of Bank of England fan chart releases “lost sight of this and reverted to focus on the central estimate.” The widening bands, however visually striking, do not automatically redirect audience attention away from the single central line.
A third concern is the illusion of precision. When forecasters make subjective, ad hoc adjustments to the width or skew of the fan without grounding those adjustments in rigorous empirical modelling of historical forecast errors, the chart “conveys a false sense of precision” that may mislead users into believing the uncertainty bounds are more solidly founded than they actually are (Bank of England, n.d.).
Disagreement in the literature. There is a stark and unresolved division in the literature regarding the ultimate utility of fan charts. On one side, prominent practitioners and researchers praise them as an “innovative” and “pioneering approach” to communicating economic uncertainty that has advanced the field substantially (Galvão et al., 2019). On the other, a recent rigorous review of the Bank of England’s own practices reaches the blunt conclusion that fan charts “have outlived their usefulness” and “have weak conceptual foundations, convey little useful information over and above what could be communicated in other, more direct ways, and receive little attention from the public” (Bank of England, n.d.). The same review recommends that fan charts “should be eliminated” from institutional communications. A second source of tension concerns whether a numerical central estimate should accompany the fan chart. The Bank of England historically refused to include a specific central number alongside the graphic — precisely to prevent audiences from ignoring the bands and focusing solely on the point estimate — a practice that attracted sustained “criticism by journalists” who found the omission evasive (Spiegelhalter et al., 2011).


10.0.3 When to Use This Method
Fan charts are best deployed when communicating with statisticians, economists, and other expert users who are mathematically equipped to interpret complex probability distribution functions and directional skewness (Galvão et al., 2019). They are conceptually most appropriate when the primary goal is to demonstrate how “the range of uncertainty inherent in the forecasts broadens with the forecast horizon” (Bank of England, n.d.) — that is, when the time-varying structure of forecast uncertainty is itself the key message. In institutional contexts such as central bank communications, where the audience includes sophisticated financial market participants who trade on probabilistic assessments of policy paths, fan charts provide a level of information that simpler formats cannot match.
Fan charts are not appropriate for entry-level audiences without extensive interpretive support, and should not be used as a stand-alone communication tool even for informed audiences. The documented failure of non-experts and journalists to engage with the uncertainty bands suggests that fan charts function best as a supplementary display accompanying plain-language uncertainty summaries, rather than as the primary mode of uncertainty communication.


10.0.4 Good Practice

Ground the fan in empirical forecast errors: To avoid conveying a false sense of precision, ensure that the baseline widths of the fan are grounded in the historical record of forecast errors for the quantity in question, rather than in subjective, ad hoc committee adjustments (Bank of England, n.d.). Documenting and disclosing the methodological basis for the bands should be standard practice.
Complement with simpler formats: Because fan charts frequently overwhelm non-expert audiences, they should not be the sole mode of uncertainty communication. For entry-level and informed audiences, supplement or replace fan charts with plain-language risk statements, simple numerical ranges, or explicit narrative accounts of the balance of risk (Bank of England, n.d.; Galvão et al., 2019).
Consider providing a central estimate: The institutional reluctance to include a numerical central estimate alongside the fan — motivated by a desire to prevent audiences from ignoring the bands — may be counterproductive if it causes audiences to disengage from the graphic entirely. Providing the central estimate explicitly, with strong textual emphasis on the uncertainty bands, may better serve comprehension than withholding it (Spiegelhalter et al., 2011).
Label probability levels explicitly: Every shaded band in the fan chart should be clearly labelled with its associated probability level, either directly on the graphic or in an immediately adjacent legend. Audiences should not be required to consult a separate methodological document to understand what each band represents.
Test with the intended audience: Given the evidence that even professional users frequently revert to the central estimate, communication products using fan charts should be tested with target audiences before deployment to verify that the uncertainty information is actually being processed and used.

title: 11  Distributions
text:
Probability distributions are the most informationally complete way to represent uncertainty about a quantity. Rather than summarising the range of possible outcomes through a single number, an interval, or a set of shaded bands, a distributional display represents the full probability assigned to every possible value of the uncertain quantity — showing not just the range but the shape, the central tendency, the skewness, and the weight of the tails. As European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019) define them, distributions are graphical representations of “probabilities for different values of an uncertain quantity that has a single true value,” most commonly presented as a Probability Density Function (PDF), a Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF), or a Complementary Cumulative Distribution Function (CCDF). Additional visual formats include density strips — narrow rectangles in which colour intensity encodes probability density — and violin plots, which combine a mirrored density display with summary statistics. As Bles et al. (2019) note, “density strips are the most accurate representation of the underlying probability distribution around the point estimate,” making them valuable when fidelity to the full distributional shape is a priority.
The distinguishing feature of all these formats is that they communicate the shape of uncertainty, not just its extent. Two forecasts might share the same 90% prediction interval yet have very different distributional shapes — one symmetrical, one heavily right-skewed; one unimodal, one bimodal. Only a distributional display can convey this distinction, and only a distributional display allows the audience to read off any quantile of interest without additional calculation. This completeness makes distributional formats the most powerful tool in the uncertainty communicator’s repertoire — and the most demanding to use responsibly.

11.0.1 Practical Example
Distributional displays have been applied in several high-stakes scientific and policy contexts. In climate science, the UK Climate Projections use paired distributions — a probability density function above a cumulative distribution function, sharing the same horizontal axis — to represent forecast uncertainty for change in maximum temperature over a specific region and time horizon (Spiegelhalter et al., 2011). This pairing of PDF and CDF allows the viewer to read both the relative likelihood of different outcomes (from the PDF) and the cumulative probability up to any threshold (from the CDF) from a single composite figure.
In food safety and agriculture, the European Food Safety Authority uses PDF graphs to communicate uncertainty derived from expert knowledge elicitation, such as the distribution “quantifying uncertainty about how many dairy cows out of a hundred on average are pregnant when slaughtered in the EU” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). These applications are notable because they involve genuine epistemic uncertainty — the product of imperfect data and expert judgement — rather than population variability, and the distributional format is used precisely to represent the full range of what is not known.
In experimental risk communication research, Edwards et al. (2012) tested the ability of lay and expert users to extract probability information from five graphical formats — PDF, CDF, CCDF, a modified CDF, and a three-dimensional PDF — using hypothetical scenarios including E. coli outbreaks, radiation treatments, airport screening, and flood depth predictions. The results of these experiments provide some of the most direct evidence available on which distributional formats best serve which communication tasks, and are drawn on extensively in the good practice recommendations below.

11.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. The primary strength of distributional displays is their informational completeness. Unlike intervals, which communicate only the extent of uncertainty, distributions provide exhaustive detail about its shape — enabling audiences to “read off approximate estimates for quantiles other than those reported explicitly” and to assess whether the distribution is symmetric, skewed, bimodal, or heavy-tailed (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). This is particularly important when the shape of the distribution carries decision- relevant information that an interval would suppress: for example, when the risk of an adverse outcome is concentrated in a heavy lower tail rather than distributed symmetrically around the central estimate.
A second strength is the capacity of distributional displays to correct motivated or uninformed misinterpretation of simpler formats. When numerical ranges are presented without distributional context, audiences frequently infer a uniform distribution — assuming that all values within the interval are equally probable. A simple density graphic accompanying an uncertainty range corrects this assumption and decreases the inter-individual variance in distributional perceptions, producing more consistent and better-calibrated interpretations across different audience members (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
A third advantage concerns the accuracy of specific probability extraction tasks. Edwards et al. (2012) found that Complementary Cumulative Distribution Functions — which plot the probability that the outcome exceeds a given value — “led to the highest probability estimation accuracy” among the formats tested, when the task required users to estimate the probability that a quantity exceeded a specified threshold.
Limitations. Against these strengths must be set several well-documented and serious limitations. The most fundamental is cognitive complexity. “Graphical representations of distributions are difficult to interpret for non-technical audiences” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019), and even technically trained users are not immune to characteristic errors. The most consistently documented is the confusion between the mean and the mode: when asked to identify the mean of a displayed PDF, audiences systematically select the mode — the peak of the distribution — “unless the mean was explicitly marked” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). Because the mean and mode coincide only for symmetric distributions, this error can produce substantially biased interpretations of skewed uncertainty distributions.
A second and more serious error concerns the use of CDFs alone. Ibrekk & Morgan (1987) found that when users were presented with a CDF without additional guidance, some were “severely misled in estimating the mean,” frequently choosing the maximum value of the distribution rather than the central estimate. The CDF’s characteristic rising shape, with its upper asymptote, appears to draw the eye to the maximum rather than the midpoint.
A third limitation is the confusion of uncertainty with variability. Because distributions are frequently used in statistics to map population variance, audiences routinely mistake epistemic uncertainty distributions — which represent a forecaster’s knowledge about a single unknown value — for descriptions of population heterogeneity (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). This misinterpretation is both common and consequential, and is difficult to eliminate through graphical means alone.
Finally, more exotic distributional formats actively harm decision-making. Edwards et al. (2012) found that three-dimensional PDFs — which add a superfluous third visual dimension to a two-dimensional quantity — “led to poor accuracy” across all measured outcomes, the worst performance of any format tested.
Disagreement in the literature. A direct empirical disagreement exists between Ibrekk & Morgan (1987) and Edwards et al. (2012) regarding the utility of CDFs for probability estimation. Ibrekk & Morgan (1987) found that CDFs allowed for “very good estimations of the probability that a value exceeds a threshold,” while Edwards et al. (2012) found that “CDFs led to poor accuracy” in similar tasks — a direct contradiction noted explicitly in European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019). This disagreement is unresolved in the literature and may reflect differences in the specific tasks tested, the populations studied, or the visual presentation of the CDFs used in each experiment. It underscores the importance of empirical testing in specific communication contexts rather than relying on general recommendations.


11.0.3 When to Use This Method
Full distributional displays — PDFs, CDFs, and related formats — should be restricted to technical-level audiences who require precise information about distributional shape, tail probabilities, or quantiles not explicitly reported in simpler summaries (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019; Spiegelhalter et al., 2011). Two-dimensional distributions, in particular, “should be used only at the technical level, and then with great care and explanation” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
The CCDF format should be preferred when the specific communication task involves helping users estimate the probability that a quantity exceeds a particular threshold — for example, the probability that a flood exceeds a specified depth, or that an emission level exceeds a regulatory limit (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
For non-technical audiences, full distributional displays should be avoided. Instead, communicators should provide the central estimate alongside a simplified prediction interval or a box plot, with explicit textual accompaniment (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). Density strips may provide a useful middle ground for informed audiences who need more than a simple interval but cannot interpret a full PDF without guidance (Bles et al., 2019).


11.0.4 Good Practice

Pair PDFs and CDFs: To mitigate the limitations of either format alone, plot a CDF directly above a PDF with the same horizontal axis scale, allowing users to cross-reference between the two representations (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). This pairing is specifically recommended as best practice by European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019) for technical-level communications.
Explicitly mark the central estimate: Never require the audience to visually estimate the mean or median from the shape of the distribution. Always clearly mark the location of the central estimate — and optionally the 5th, 25th, 75th, and 95th percentiles — on both curves in a paired display (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Accompany with textual explanation: Distributional displays must not stand alone. They should always be accompanied by sufficient textual explanation, in the simplest terms possible, that explicitly defines what the height and area of the curves represent and what the viewer should take away from the graphic (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Clarify uncertainty versus variability: Because audiences routinely misinterpret epistemic uncertainty distributions as descriptions of population variability, the accompanying text must explicitly state that “the distribution represents uncertainty about the quantity of interest, for which there is a single true value” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Avoid three-dimensional formats: Three-dimensional PDF displays add visual complexity without informational benefit and actively impair decision-making accuracy. They should not be used in any communication context (Edwards et al., 2012).
Use density strips for compact density communication: When the full distributional display is too complex for the target audience but simple intervals are too sparse, density strips provide an effective intermediate format that conveys distributional shape in a compact, visually accessible form (Bles et al., 2019).

title: 12  Prediction Intervals and Quantiles
text:
Prediction intervals and quantiles represent uncertainty by identifying specific points or bounded ranges in the probability distribution of a future outcome — communicating not the full shape of the distribution but the most decision-relevant summaries of its extent. A prediction interval specifies a range within which a future observation is expected to fall with a stated probability: a 90% prediction interval, for instance, is constructed to contain the true future value on approximately 90% of occasions when the forecast is made under similar conditions (Raftery, 2016). A quantile specifies a threshold below which a given proportion of possible outcomes are expected to fall: a forecast at the 0.65 quantile, for example, “will estimate a value that is lower than the observed value 65% of the time” (Unknown Author, n.d.).
These two closely related concepts can be presented visually — most commonly through error bars, box plots, or shaded bands on time series plots — or numerically, as explicit lower and upper bounds accompanying a central estimate. In a standard box plot, a central rectangle represents the middle quartiles (the 25th to 75th percentile range), a line inside the box indicates the median, and extending whiskers represent upper and lower probability bounds (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). In time series forecasting, prediction intervals are typically displayed as a shaded region surrounding the central forecast line, widening as the horizon extends to reflect the accumulation of uncertainty over time.
The relationship between prediction intervals and the full distributional displays described in Chapter 11 is one of summarisation: a prediction interval extracts two specific quantiles from the full distribution and presents them without the distributional shape that lies between and beyond them. This compression is precisely what makes intervals practical for non-expert audiences — and precisely what creates their characteristic limitations, as the information that is discarded by the compression is not always negligible.

12.0.1 Practical Example
Prediction intervals have been applied across a wide range of forecasting contexts. In weather forecasting, the University of Washington’s Probcast website communicated temperature uncertainty to the general public using the 10th and 90th percentiles of the forecast distribution — an 80% prediction interval — alongside the probability of specific adverse weather events (Raftery, 2016). The choice of 80% rather than 95% intervals was deliberate: while 95% intervals contain more true outcomes, they are “also much wider and hence may be judged less useful” for practical decision-making by users planning outdoor activities or making weather-sensitive choices (Raftery, 2016).
In epidemiological forecasting during the COVID-19 pandemic, 95% prediction intervals were used to present probabilistic bounds around time-series forecasts of hospital bed occupancy, providing health system managers with an upper bound for capacity planning (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). In demographic forecasting, Raftery & Ševčíková (2023) use prediction intervals around Total Fertility Rate projections to demonstrate how forecast uncertainty is “narrow for short-term predictions, then widens and finally stabilises as the forecast horizon increases” — a pattern that is directly legible from the interval display without requiring the viewer to interpret a full distributional shape.
In intelligence analysis, Mandel & Irwin (2021) advocate for replacing vague linguistic probability terms with explicit numeric credible intervals: reporting that there is a “65% to 85% probability that a given event will occur” is more precise, more verifiable, and less susceptible to motivated misinterpretation than verbal alternatives such as “likely.”
In retail and supply chain management, the quantile framework is applied directly to asymmetric cost structures: when “the cost of being understocked is often higher than the cost of being overstocked,” planning at the 75th quantile rather than the median is more economically appropriate, because it provides protection against the higher-cost error at an acceptable frequency (Unknown Author, n.d.).

12.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Prediction intervals have several important practical advantages. First, they facilitate risk management under conditions of asymmetric costs. Because quantiles can be specified to match the cost structure of a particular decision — providing more protection against the more costly type of error — they enable decision-makers to optimise their planning in a way that a simple central estimate cannot support (Unknown Author, n.d.). The “risk avoider” identified by Raftery (2016) — a user whose primary goal is to limit the probability of an adverse outcome below an acceptable threshold — finds a specific quantile far more actionable than the full distributional information from which it is derived.
Second, visualising uncertainty through box plots and error bars enables users to extract mean estimates and make subsequent decisions with higher accuracy than complex distributional displays. Edwards et al. (2012) found that “error bar displays and box plots work best for mean estimation and choice of action” — a finding that supports the use of intervals precisely in contexts where simplicity and actionability are priorities.
Third, communicating uncertainty through numerical ranges with a point estimate does not significantly reduce public trust in the source. Bles et al. (2020) demonstrate that this format “does not substantially decrease trust in either the numbers or the source” — directly addressing one of the most common concerns among institutional communicators who worry that expressing uncertainty will damage their credibility.
Fourth, quantile-based communication offers a practical solution to the challenge of expert knowledge communication. Raftery (2016) demonstrate that a single, well-chosen quantile can serve as the entire actionable output of a complex probabilistic analysis — as in the International Whaling Commission’s use of the fifth percentile of the bowhead whale replacement yield distribution as a precautionary quota limit — delivering probabilistically principled decisions without requiring the audience to interpret a full distribution.
Limitations. The most persistent and well-documented limitation of prediction intervals is what Toet et al. (2016) call the uniform distribution fallacy: when numeric intervals or error bars are presented without explicit information about distributional shape, audiences routinely assume a uniform distribution, incorrectly believing that all values within the range are equally probable. As Spiegelhalter et al. (2011) describe it, the visual presentation of an interval makes it appear as though “values within the interval are equally likely,” encouraging users to anchor on the extreme bounds rather than the central estimate — even though those extremes represent the least likely outcomes within the interval.
A related limitation is excessive focus on the extremes. Because the bounds of an interval are visually salient — they are the edges of a bar or the endpoints of a whisker — users tend to anchor on them, producing a systematic bias toward the consideration of extreme scenarios at the expense of the more probable central outcomes (Spiegelhalter et al., 2011).
A third limitation is the deterministic construal error in its interval- specific form: audiences frequently interpret the boundaries of a prediction interval as strict categorical partitions, perceiving values within the interval as “accurate” and values outside it as “erroneous” — as if the interval were a binary classifier rather than a probabilistic statement (Leffrang & Müller, 2025). This misinterpretation can lead to inappropriate surprise and loss of trust when an actual outcome falls outside a stated interval, even if that outcome had a non-trivial probability.
Finally, because box plots and ranges are also widely used to display population variability, audiences routinely misinterpret epistemic uncertainty intervals as descriptions of real-world heterogeneity — the same confusion documented for distributional displays in Chapter 11.
Disagreement in the literature. The literature reveals a historical disagreement about how numeric intervals affect perceived competence and trust. Bles et al. (2020) conclude from meta-analysis that providing numeric ranges does not negatively affect trust, while earlier research by Johnson and Slovic (referenced in Bles et al. (2020)) found that interval communication “signalled honesty and competency for some participants, but dishonesty and incompetency for others.” A second tension concerns the visual design of interval boundaries: while traditional statistical graphics use sharp, crisp boundary lines for intervals, Toet et al. (2016) argue that “blurred or dashed (versus sharp) borders” would better convey that values become gradually less probable as they approach the extremes of the interval, reducing the deterministic construal error. This recommendation has not yet been widely adopted in practice.


12.0.3 When to Use This Method
Prediction intervals and quantile forecasts are appropriate across a wide range of audiences and contexts, making them among the most versatile methods in the uncertainty communicator’s toolkit. For risk avoiders who need to guarantee an outcome up to a specific probability threshold, a specific quantile — chosen to match the cost structure of the decision — is the appropriate output (Raftery, 2016). For mass-media communications regarding scientific facts, numerical intervals with a central point estimate represent “the most robust format for conveying uncertainty without confusing the general public” (Bles et al., 2020).
The choice of coverage probability matters. Raftery (2016) recommend 80% prediction intervals over the conventional 95% for public-facing communications, on the grounds that narrower intervals are more useful for practical decision-making even though they contain fewer actual outcomes. For high-stakes decisions where the tail risk is the primary concern — flood protection, capacity planning, infrastructure resilience — wider intervals or specific extreme quantiles may be more appropriate.
Intervals should be preferred over full distributional displays when the audience is at the entry or informed level, when the communication context does not support the cognitive load of distributional interpretation, or when the specific decision can be mapped onto a single probability threshold.


12.0.4 Good Practice

Always state the associated probability explicitly: Never present a numeric range in isolation. Always pair the range with a clear statement of the probability that the quantity falls within it — for example, “there is a 90% probability that the outcome will fall between X and Y” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Always provide the central estimate: Accompany every interval with a median or mean estimate to counter the tendency of users to focus on the extremes and to signal whether the distribution is symmetric or skewed (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Incorporate density cues where possible: To reduce the uniform distribution fallacy, supplement intervals or box plots with a visual density strip or explicit text explaining that central values are more probable than extreme values (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Consider blurred or dashed interval borders: To reduce the deterministic construal error, explore the use of visual treatments — such as gradual fading at the interval boundaries — that signal the continuous nature of the probability distribution beyond the stated bounds (Toet et al., 2016).
Communicate interval bounds explicitly in text: Do not require audiences to read the bounds of an interval from a graphical axis. Always state the upper and lower bounds numerically in accompanying text so that the precise values are unambiguous (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Always accompany box plots with explanations: Because box plots are frequently misunderstood, include a textual or graphical key that explicitly defines what the whiskers, box, and central line represent, and reiterate that the graphic represents epistemic uncertainty about a single quantity rather than population variability (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Match the quantile to the decision: When advising specific users rather than a general audience, choose the quantile to communicate based on the cost structure of their decision — protecting against the high-cost error at an appropriate frequency rather than defaulting to the conventional 50th or 95th percentile (Raftery, 2016; Unknown Author, n.d.).

title: 13  Categorical Approaches
text:
Categorical approaches communicate uncertainty by classifying data, model outputs, or expert assessments into discrete, ordered qualitative tiers — such as low, medium, and high — or by employing visual symbolic systems that encode these tiers through colour, icon, or label. The most widely recognised example is the Red-Amber-Green (RAG) traffic light system, in which colours convey an ordered progression from acceptable to problematic (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). Other instances include national terrorism threat level scales, IPCC confidence grids, ecological risk matrices, and data pedigree charts that rate the quality of scientific evidence. What unites all these systems is the act of discretisation: continuous probabilistic information, or complex multi- dimensional uncertainty, is compressed into a small number of mutually exclusive categories that are easier to communicate, easier to remember, and easier to act upon — at the cost of the quantitative precision and nuance that the underlying information may possess.
Categorical systems serve a fundamentally different communicative function from the quantitative methods reviewed in the preceding sections. Rather than attempting to convey the magnitude or shape of uncertainty, they aim to convey its significance: whether the level of uncertainty associated with a particular assessment warrants concern, caution, or confidence. As Bles et al. (2019) observe, “indirect uncertainty” — uncertainty about the quality, completeness, or consensus underlying an assessment — is “possibly best amalgamated into a qualitative or ordered categorical scale,” precisely because such uncertainty does not lend itself to numerical expression in the first place.

13.0.1 Practical Example
Categorical approaches are deployed across a strikingly diverse range of domains. In climate risk assessment, the DNV recommended practice for simulation models uses a categorical risk matrix that evaluates the danger of a model failing by combining an uncertainty categorisation — Low, Medium, High — with an assessment of the severity of consequences — Minor to Catastrophic — and mapping the combined result onto a colour-coded “traffic light” grid ranging from green (trusted) to red (not trusted) (Pascoe et al., n.d.). This two-dimensional categorical framework communicates both the degree of uncertainty and its practical importance in a single compact display.
In ecological risk assessment, probabilistic Bayesian network analyses of environmental chemical exposures map the probability of Risk Quotient intervals onto a visual categorical scale where “colours range from green (no risk) to red (posing a risk)” (Moe et al., 2022). This approach bridges the gap between quantitative probabilistic analysis and actionable risk communication, by translating continuous probability outputs into categories that regulatory decision-makers can act on directly.
In intelligence analysis and national security, the US National Intelligence Council communicates analytic confidence using a categorical “high/medium/low scale,” while the UK defines its national Terrorism Threat Level through a categorical scale with verbal labels — where “SEVERE” is defined as a highly likely attack — that is communicated directly to the public through media and government channels (Spiegelhalter, 2017). In science quality assessment, the NUSAP (Numeral, Unit, Spread, Assessment, Pedigree) notational system evaluates the mode of production and quality of scientific information, which “can be represented as a spider or radar chart using traffic light symbols — green for good quality, red for low quality” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Finally, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change uses a categorical two-dimensional confidence grid that combines a verbal assessment of evidence strength (“limited,” “medium,” “robust”) with a verbal assessment of scientific agreement (“low,” “medium,” “high”) to communicate the overall confidence in specific findings (Mastrandrea et al., 2010).

(2010), which maps combinations of evidence strength and agreement level onto a categorical confidence scale from “low confidence” to “high confidence,” providing a widely used template for categorical meta-uncertainty communication.

13.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Categorical approaches offer two distinctive strengths that quantitative methods cannot replicate. The first is accessibility: evaluative labels such as “high,” “moderate,” and “low” are accessible to lay audiences and can “highlight aspects of uncertainty information that may otherwise be overlooked in more complex numerical displays” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). For audiences with limited statistical literacy, a traffic light system communicates the essential decision-relevant information — is this safe or not? should I be concerned? — without requiring any interpretation of probability values.
The second strength is their suitability for expressing what might be called unquantifiable or indirect uncertainty — the kind that cannot legitimately be assigned a precise numerical probability because it concerns the quality, completeness, or contested nature of the underlying knowledge rather than the probability of a specific outcome. As Spiegelhalter (2017) notes, such “unmodelled uncertainty can only be expressed as a qualitative and subjective assessment.” When the goal is to communicate the pedigree of evidence, the degree of scientific consensus, or the confidence in a model rather than the probability of a specific event, categorical scales are not merely simpler than quantitative formats — they are more epistemically honest.
Limitations. Against these strengths must be set several well-documented and serious limitations. The most significant is what European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019) describe as the “double-edged sword” of evaluative labels: categorical labels can inadvertently signal to a layperson that the categorised element is the only thing that matters, causing them to fixate on the label and “ignore other ways of looking at uncertainty like examining upper and lower values.” By directing attention so powerfully to the category, the system may inadvertently suppress consideration of the quantitative information from which the category was derived.
A second limitation is the conflation of likelihood and confidence. Qualitative categorical scales routinely blur the crucial distinction between the probability of an outcome occurring and the assessor’s confidence in that estimate, because “likelihood implicitly contains confidence levels” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). A risk assessed as “high” might be high because it is likely or because the analysts are very confident it will occur at moderate frequency — two very different epistemic states that the categorical label cannot distinguish.
A third limitation is ambiguity and lack of standardisation. Most categorical scales lack formal operational definitions of the type that exist for probability itself, leading to inconsistent interpretation across different audiences, contexts, and time periods (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). The word “possible” in one categorical framework may correspond to a 20–30% probability; in another, it may indicate any outcome that cannot be excluded. Without explicit quantitative anchoring, categorical labels are susceptible to motivated interpretation.
A fourth limitation is information loss. By forcing continuous probabilistic data into discrete categorical buckets, the system “limits users’ ability to obtain the information that may be most useful” (National Research Council, 2006). All values within a category are treated as equivalent for communication purposes, even if they differ substantially in their underlying probabilities or consequences.
A fifth concern, documented through empirical testing, is that some categorical symbol systems are actively harmful. Attempts to use +/– tables to convey the direction of uncertainty have been found “confusing” by both technical and lay audiences, who “misinterpreted the +/– signs as cancelling each other out” — a response described as finding them “incomprehensible” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Disagreement in the literature. The literature highlights a persistent and unresolved conflict between user preferences and comprehension accuracy. Certain professional audiences — including aviation forecasters and industry stakeholders — strongly prefer categorical simplifications because they feel intuitive and actionable (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019; National Research Council, 2006). Yet empirical research “consensually criticises the use of verbal uncertainty expressions alone, which can be responsible for significant misunderstanding” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). The preference for categories appears to reflect their communicative convenience rather than their epistemic accuracy, suggesting that the widespread use of categorical systems may be driven by communication comfort rather than communication effectiveness.


13.0.3 When to Use This Method
Categorical approaches are most appropriate when the goal is to communicate meta-uncertainty — the overall quality, strength, or consensus of the underlying evidence — rather than the precise probability of a specific outcome (Bles et al., 2019; Spiegelhalter, 2017). They are also appropriate when the uncertainty being communicated is genuinely unquantifiable — for example, when expressing the confidence in a model’s structural assumptions, the pedigree of data, or the degree of expert consensus — because in such cases a categorical scale is not an approximation of a more precise quantity but the most honest available representation of the state of knowledge.
Categorical scales should be restricted to “unambiguous events” to prevent audiences from confusing the linguistic ambiguity of the event description with the uncertainty about its probability (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). They should always be supplemented with quantitative information where available, and should never serve as the primary or sole means of communicating numerical probability estimates.


13.0.4 Good Practice

Explicitly define all cut-offs: When using evaluative categories such as “high,” “moderate,” and “low,” ensure that the quantitative thresholds for each category are precisely defined and the rationale for the chosen thresholds is transparently explained (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). Vague categorical labels whose boundaries are undefined invite inconsistent interpretation.
Provide quantitative translations: Any qualitative category or symbol must be explicitly paired with a quantitative definition of its meaning every time it is used — not only in a technical appendix (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). A table hidden in a footnote is insufficient; the quantitative translation should appear directly alongside the categorical label in the main communication.
Supplement with numeric scales where appropriate: Following the InterAcademy Council’s recommendations to the IPCC, qualitative confidence scales “may be supplemented by a quantitative probability scale, if appropriate” (Mastrandrea et al., 2010). This supplementation preserves the accessibility of the categorical display while protecting against the information loss that discretisation inevitably introduces.
Avoid +/– symbol systems: Given the documented confusion and misinterpretation associated with directional symbol tables, communicators should not use +/– notation to convey uncertainty direction. If directional information is important, convey it through explicit text or through asymmetric visual displays (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Use two-dimensional categorical matrices with caution: Two-dimensional risk matrices that combine uncertainty with consequence severity — such as the DNV risk matrix — provide more information than one-dimensional categorical scales, but also impose greater cognitive demands. Ensure that both axes are clearly labelled and that the colour scheme is accessible to colour-blind users.
Test categorical labels empirically: Because the interpretation of categorical labels is highly context-dependent and can vary substantially across audiences, test the chosen labels with representative members of the target audience before deployment, rather than assuming that terms like “moderate” or “significant” have a shared meaning.

title: 14  Scenario and What-If Analysis
text:
Scenario and what-if analyses are methods for exploring possible futures without sampling from an underlying probability distribution — making them fundamentally distinct from the frequency-based probabilistic approaches reviewed in earlier sections. Where prediction intervals, fan charts, and distributional displays express uncertainty as a probability assigned to different values of a continuous quantity, scenario analysis instead develops “a set of possible futures based on extrapolating current trends and varying key parameters, without sampling in a systematic manner from an uncertainty distribution” (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2015). The scenarios produced by this method are not random draws from a probability model; they are deliberately constructed, internally consistent narratives about how the future might unfold under different assumptions about the key drivers of change.
In strategic planning and organisational foresight, scenarios are described as “purposeful stories about how the contextual environment could unfold over time,” composed of internally consistent causal accounts of how different variables — social, technological, economic, political, and environmental — interact and co-evolve (Wright & Goodwin, 2009). Each scenario represents one coherent version of a possible future, designed not to be the most likely outcome but to be a plausible and internally consistent one that illuminates a specific combination of uncertainties. In algorithmic forecasting, what-if analysis takes a more specific and quantitative form: it is “a scenario modelling technique where you make a hypothetical change to a time series and compare the forecasts generated by these changes against the baseline” (Unknown Author, n.d.), allowing decision-makers to see directly how different choices or conditions would affect projected outcomes.
The shared feature of both approaches is their deliberate agnosticism about probability. By presenting a small number of distinctly different possible futures without attaching explicit probabilities to each, scenario analysis communicates the range of plausibility rather than the probability distribution of outcomes — a distinction that is both its greatest strength and the source of its most characteristic misuse.

14.0.1 Practical Example
Scenario and what-if analysis have been applied across a remarkably wide range of domains. In climate policy, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change uses scenarios — currently presented as Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) — to represent different emission trajectories and model the Earth’s possible responses under each (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2015). The Royal Netherlands Meteorological Institute (KNMI) communicates climate risks to policy advisers and the public using a four-quadrant framework that intersects two key axes of uncertainty — low versus high CO2 emissions, and wetter versus drier climates — to produce four clearly differentiated scenarios for the future climate of the Netherlands (Pascoe et al., n.d.).
In public health, preparations for unknown future disease outbreaks are formalised through scenario or table-top exercises that envisage catastrophic possibilities, trace their implications through the health system, and test the robustness of response protocols against genuinely uncertain threats (Scoones, 2019). In social science research, Komp-Leukkunen (2020) applies scenario-based foresighting to map how the social integration of refugees might evolve under different hypothetical immigration policy frameworks — a domain where neither historical precedent nor probability models can adequately capture the range of possible futures.
In retail and commercial forecasting, algorithmic what-if analysis tools allow retailers to simulate the effect of specific business decisions — for example, examining how a 25% markdown on all clothing items, or an extension of a sale’s duration by two weeks, would affect baseline inventory and revenue projections relative to a no-change scenario (Unknown Author, n.d.).

(n.d.), displaying the KNMI’23 scenarios for climate change in the Netherlands using a four-quadrant graphical framework, with the two axes representing CO2 emission level (low/high) and precipitation change (wetter/drier), and the four resulting scenarios occupying each quadrant.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission from Unknown Author (n.d.) or recreate from a comparable publicly available forecasting tool interface.





14.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Scenario and what-if analysis offer three distinct advantages over probabilistic methods that make them the appropriate tool in specific circumstances.
The first and most fundamental is the ability to circumvent the problem of unquantifiable probability. Scenario analysis “offers an approach for handling situations of low predictability which avoids the requirement to make such estimates” (Wright & Goodwin, 2009) — that is, it enables rigorous planning under conditions of deep uncertainty where the assignment of reliable, calibrated probabilities is either impossible or would constitute a misleading pretence of precision. By constructing detailed, coherent possible futures without claiming to know their relative probabilities, scenario analysis is more epistemically honest than probabilistic methods in genuinely uncertain domains.
The second advantage is the capacity for collective sense-making and risk discovery. By building narrative storylines that trace the causal interactions between different drivers of change, scenario analysis “brings latent issues to the surface, so that it is more difficult to deny the prospect of high- impact events” (Wright & Goodwin, 2009). This social function of scenario planning is distinct from its analytical function: the process of constructing and discussing scenarios within a team or stakeholder group forces the explicit articulation of assumptions, surfaces hidden disagreements about how the world works, and makes it harder for high-consequence risks to be dismissed without engagement.
The third advantage is the promotion of adaptive strategy. By exploring divergent possible futures, scenario analysis encourages decision-makers to test the robustness of their plans across multiple eventualities, generating flexible strategies that remain effective across a range of possible outcomes rather than optimised for a single predicted scenario (Pascoe et al., n.d.). This is particularly valuable in long-horizon planning where structural changes in the system — demographic shifts, technological transitions, political realignments — are likely to render any single-point forecast obsolete.
Limitations. Against these genuine strengths, the literature documents several characteristic limitations and failure modes.
The most significant is deterministic misuse: analysts and end-users frequently forget that a scenario represents only one specific, internally consistent combination of variables, with a very low probability of exact occurrence. They instead use a single scenario as a deterministic forecast of what will happen, “without recognising that they have a low probability of occurrence” (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2015). This misuse is especially common when organisations select one scenario from a set and build all their planning around it, treating the selection as a prediction rather than as one plausible future among several.
A second limitation is future myopia — the tendency of scenario builders to generate futures that are variations on themes that participants already find familiar and plausible. Because scenarios are constructed using the existing mental models of the participants, they regularly fail to incorporate “unknown unknowns” or events without obvious, immediate causes (Wright & Goodwin, 2009). Participants in scenario workshops “tend to regularly emphasise economic factors” and other domains where they feel competent, systematically underweighting social, cultural, or technological disruptions that fall outside their professional frame.
A third limitation concerns the simulation heuristic and its paradoxical effects. While scenario planning is broadly designed to break decision-makers out of narrow historical framings, Wright & Goodwin (2009) identify a paradox: the act of actively constructing detailed causal chains can trigger the simulation heuristic, causing participants to become inappropriately overconfident in the specific scenario they have just built. Rather than opening up the space of possibilities, the construction process may reinforce existing framings and “produce increased, but inappropriate, confidence in one’s ability to anticipate the future” (Wright & Goodwin, 2009).
A fourth limitation applies specifically to algorithmic what-if analysis: when the hypothetical scenario involves conditions entirely outside the historical range of the training data, machine learning-based forecasting models may “not be accurate for novel scenarios where there is no reference available to determine whether a forecast is good” (Unknown Author, n.d.). The model’s ability to generate a number for any scenario should not be mistaken for its ability to generate a reliable number.


14.0.3 When to Use This Method
Scenario analysis is most appropriate under conditions of “deep uncertainty,” “ambiguity,” or “ignorance,” where fundamental outcomes are poorly defined, probabilities have a weak or non-existent empirical basis, and future predictability is low (Pascoe et al., n.d.). Stirling (n.d.) explicitly maps scenario analysis to the domain of ambiguity in his incertitude framework — situations where possible outcomes are contested or framed differently by different stakeholders — making it the most honest available approach when quantitative probabilities cannot be legitimately assigned.
It should be employed when the forecast horizon is sufficiently long that structural changes in the system — such as economic development trajectories, demographic shifts, or technological transitions — become the dominant drivers of outcomes (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2015). In such contexts, the scenario approach explicitly acknowledges what a probabilistic forecast would falsely conceal: that the future depends fundamentally on choices that have not yet been made.
Scenario analysis is less appropriate when the decision requires a specific probability estimate for risk management purposes, when the audience needs a single actionable threshold, or when the scenarios risk being misused as deterministic predictions.


14.0.4 Good Practice

Present an even number of scenarios: To prevent audiences from exhibiting a cognitive bias toward treating the “middle” scenario as the most likely, present an even number of scenarios — typically four — so that there is no natural central option to anchor on (Pascoe et al., n.d.).
Use backward logic to generate scenarios: To combat future myopia and generate genuinely diverse and challenging possibilities, work backwards from extreme objectives or failure states and deduce the causal drivers required to reach them, rather than extrapolating forward from the present (Wright & Goodwin, 2009). This “fault tree” approach is more likely to surface uncomfortable but important possibilities.
Explicitly state that scenarios are not predictions: Every scenario communication should include a clear, prominent statement that the scenarios represent possible futures, not forecasts of what will happen, and that they do not have assigned probabilities (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2015). This disclaimer should appear in the body of the communication, not merely in a methodological appendix.
Incorporate historical variability as a reference: When presenting scenarios to public or informed-level audiences, include graphical representations of historical year-to-year variability alongside the projected scenario range, to give audiences a tangible reference point for the magnitude of projected changes (Pascoe et al., n.d.).
Incorporate stakeholder motivations: Ensure that the behavioural responses embedded in scenarios are grounded in an understanding of what key stakeholders are likely to do when facing the conditions described in each scenario (Wright & Goodwin, 2009). Scenarios that do not account for how institutions and individuals will respond to the changes they describe are incomplete and may generate misleading strategic conclusions.
Combine with probabilistic methods where possible: In contexts where some quantitative basis for probability assignment exists, scenario analysis can be combined with probabilistic methods — for example, by assigning indicative probability ranges to each scenario or by using probabilistic models to explore the consequences of each scenario — producing a richer and more actionable uncertainty communication than either approach alone.

title: 15  Verbal Expressions of Uncertainty
text:
Verbal expressions of uncertainty use natural language words and phrases — such as “likely,” “possible,” “doubtful,” “almost certain,” or “slight hope” — to convey the probability of an event or the confidence in a scientific assessment without relying exclusively on numerical metrics. These expressions draw on the full richness of linguistic probability — the repertoire of words that human language has evolved to convey degrees of belief, confidence, and expectation — and they are the oldest and most instinctive mode of uncertainty communication available. Natural language expressions may be used alone, with modifiers such as “very” or “extremely,” or with hedges such as “almost” or “probably” that qualify their directional intent (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). In policy and intelligence contexts, standardised sets of verbal expressions are formally codified as “words of estimative probability” — prescribed lexicons that assign specific verbal terms to specific probability ranges with the goal of reducing interpretive variability across analysts and institutions (Mandel & Irwin, 2021).
The appeal of verbal expressions is immediate and intuitive: they are the mode of communication through which all human beings first learn to express uncertainty, and they remain dominant in everyday risk communication from medical consultations to weather forecasts to news reporting. Yet the literature on verbal uncertainty communication is among the most consistently critical in the field, documenting a pattern of systematic misinterpretation, variable interpretation, and audience alienation that places severe limits on the use of verbal expressions as a standalone communication format.

15.0.1 Practical Example
The historical benchmark for verbal probability communication — and for its failure — is Sherman Kent’s 1951 National Intelligence Estimate on Yugoslavia. The estimate used the term “serious possibility” to describe the likelihood of a Soviet attack on Yugoslavia. When Kent subsequently surveyed the analysts who had contributed to the estimate, he discovered that they interpreted the same phrase wildly differently: Kent himself took “serious possibility” to mean approximately a 65% probability, while other analysts gave estimates ranging from 20% to 80% (Mandel & Irwin, 2021). This 60-percentage-point spread in the interpretation of a single phrase prompted Kent to advocate for the standardisation of verbal probability lexicons in intelligence work — an effort that continues today. Modern intelligence agencies including the US Office of the Director of National Intelligence (ODNI), the UK Professional Head of Intelligence Assessment (PHIA), and NATO all maintain standardised verbal scales that map prescribed words onto defined numerical probability ranges (Mandel & Irwin, 2021).
In climate science, the IPCC employs a highly calibrated verbal likelihood scale — including terms such as “virtually certain” (99–100%), “very likely” (90–100%), “likely” (66–100%), “about as likely as not” (33–66%), “unlikely” (0–33%), and “exceptionally unlikely” (0–1%) — to communicate the likelihood of specific future climate outcomes in its assessment reports (Mastrandrea et al., 2010). This scale is among the most carefully designed verbal probability lexicons in science, with each term explicitly defined and consistently applied across thousands of findings across multiple working groups.
In meteorology, weather forecasters routinely use linguistic probability expressions — “slight chance,” “likely,” “a good chance” — to indicate the probability of precipitation or severe weather events in public forecasts (Ripberger et al., 2022). In legal proceedings, the phrase “beyond reasonable doubt” functions as a verbal expression of high certainty, though empirical research shows that jurors interpret it across a very wide numerical range.

Suggested figure: Figure 1 in Dhami & Mandel (2022), which maps the verbal probability lexicons used by major intelligence agencies — Kent & Foster, ODNI, PHIA, and NATO — onto a common 0% to 100% numeric scale, enabling direct visual comparison of how different institutions have attempted to standardise verbal probability communication.

Figure placeholder — IPCC Likelihood Scale
