Figure placeholder — Public Interpretation of Verbal Probability Terms
Suggested figure: Figure 2.5 in National Research Council (2006), a bar chart visually plotting the wide, overlapping ranges of numerical interpretations that members of the public actually assign to common verbal uncertainty terms such as “Possible,” “Good Chance,” and “Probable,” illustrating the empirical extent of interpretive variability.
Action needed: Reproduce with permission from National Research Council (2006).





15.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Verbal expressions have three genuine strengths that explain their persistence across virtually all domains of uncertainty communication.
The first is naturalness and cognitive ease. People “find it easier and more natural to use words than numbers” when expressing probability, because language is acquired earlier and is the dominant mode of all human communication (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). For senders — the scientists, analysts, and practitioners who generate uncertainty assessments — verbal expressions reduce the cognitive burden of communication and allow nuance to be expressed without the precision that numbers imply.
The second is directionality — a feature of verbal probability expressions that numeric formats cannot replicate. Verbal probability terms possess inherent directional orientation: positively phrased terms (e.g., “small probability of success”) draw attention to the occurrence of an event, while negatively phrased terms (e.g., “unlikely to succeed”) draw attention to its non-occurrence (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). This directionality allows communicators to implicitly guide the audience’s attention toward or away from specific outcomes, providing a framing tool that goes beyond the bare expression of probability.
The third is face-saving flexibility. Verbal expressions are inherently less precise than numbers, and this imprecision can be used strategically: senders can “use terms as a receiver- or self-face-saving strategy, particularly when communicating the chances of adverse outcomes” (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). When a prediction turns out to be wrong, the vagueness of a verbal expression provides insulation against accountability claims in a way that a stated numerical probability does not.
Limitations. Against these strengths must be set four serious and consistently documented limitations.
The most fundamental is imprecision and interpretive variability. The literature “consensually criticises the use of verbal uncertainty expressions alone, which can be responsible for significant misunderstanding” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). The numerical interpretation of common verbal probability terms varies enormously across individuals: the word “possible,” for instance, spans up to 63 percentage points in the interpretations given by surveyed physicians (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). The phrase “beyond reasonable doubt” shows similar variability among jurors. This variability is not random — it is systematic, influenced by the severity of the outcome described, the context of the communication, and the individual’s prior beliefs — and it cannot be eliminated by encouragement to interpret words consistently.
The second limitation is the severity effect: audiences consistently interpret the same verbal probability term as numerically higher when the event it describes is more severe or undesirable (Ripberger et al., 2022). A doctor who says a treatment has a “slight chance” of causing harm may find that patients interpret this as a substantially higher probability than if the same phrase were used to describe a minor inconvenience. This context sensitivity means that verbal expressions cannot be relied upon to convey consistent probability information across different content domains.
The third limitation is the extremity effect: certain linguistic terms drive audiences to assume extreme outcomes. The word “improbable,” for example, is frequently interpreted as meaning impossible — a 0% probability — rather than a low but non-negligible frequency (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019; Ripberger et al., 2022). This error is particularly consequential for risk communication about rare but important events: calling a Black Swan event “improbable” may cause audiences to discount it entirely rather than planning for it at a low level of probability.
The fourth limitation is integration failure: it is cognitively difficult to mathematically combine multiple verbal probability expressions, leading to lower logical coherence and worse aggregation accuracy than numerical formats (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). When a decision requires integrating uncertainty information from multiple sources — each expressed verbally — the resulting judgement is likely to be poorly calibrated relative to what numerical integration would produce.
Disagreement in the literature. The literature reveals a sharp empirical disagreement regarding whether verbal expressions protect or damage communicator credibility. Dhami & Mandel (2022) find that senders who use verbal terms that are directionally consistent with an unexpected outcome — for example, a positive verbal term for a positive outcome that did not occur — “suffered less credibility loss” after a wrong prediction, because the linguistic vagueness shielded them from precise accountability. This suggests that verbal expressions can function as a credibility-protection device.
However, direct experimental evidence from Bles et al. (2020) points in the opposite direction: adding verbal uncertainty caveats to news articles “reduced participants’ trust in the source” compared to using numerical ranges or no uncertainty qualification at all. Verbal hedging, in this study, actively damaged the communicator’s credibility rather than protecting it.
A second disagreement concerns the persistent tension between sender preferences and receiver needs. Forecasters and analysts strongly prefer verbal expressions precisely because of their face-saving properties (National Research Council, 2006), while policymakers and the public consistently prefer numerical precision for high-stakes decisions (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). This sender-receiver mismatch is not merely a difference in stylistic preference: it represents a systematic misalignment between the incentives of communicators and the needs of their audiences.


15.0.3 When to Use This Method
Verbal expressions are best used to summarise the overall quality, strength, or limitations of underlying evidence — what the literature terms meta- uncertainty — where precise quantitative probabilities cannot be legitimately calculated (Bles et al., 2019). They are also effective when the communicator needs to explicitly convey directionality — signalling that a risk is increasing relative to a baseline, or that a finding is stronger than previously reported — rather than a specific probability value (Ripberger et al., 2022).
Verbal expressions should never be used as the sole mode of communicating quantitative probability information when a numerical estimate is available and when the audience needs to make a consequential decision based on the probability (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). In such contexts, they should be used only as a supplement to numerical information, not as a replacement for it.


15.0.4 Good Practice

Always pair verbal expressions with numerical translations: Never use a verbal probability expression in isolation. Always include a specific numerical probability or range directly adjacent to the verbal term (Ripberger et al., 2022). The numerical value should appear first, with the verbal label serving as a mnemonic aid rather than the primary communication.
Present the number before the word: When combining numerical and verbal formats, present the numerical probability before the verbal term — for example, “20% — unlikely” rather than “unlikely (20%)” — to anchor the audience’s interpretation in the number and reduce the extremity effect associated with the verbal label (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Avoid “improbable” and “unlikely” for rare events: Because non-expert audiences routinely interpret these terms as meaning impossible (0%), communicators should avoid them when describing rare but non-negligible events. Instead, use positively framed terms such as “low probability” or “a small but real chance” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Avoid vague hedging words: Avoid qualifiers such as “about,” “around,” “possible,” or “there is a chance,” as these maximise interpretive variability and degrade comprehension without adding communicative value (Ripberger et al., 2022).
Use standardised lexicons where they exist: In domains such as climate science (IPCC likelihood scale) and intelligence analysis (ODNI, PHIA, NATO scales), use the established standardised verbal probability lexicons consistently and cite their numerical definitions explicitly. Deviation from established scales introduces ambiguity that standardisation was designed to eliminate (Mandel & Irwin, 2021).
Test interpretation empirically: Before deploying verbal probability expressions in a specific communication context, test the intended audience’s numerical interpretation of the chosen terms. Given the documented variability in interpretation, terms that seem obviously clear to the communicator may be systematically misunderstood by the audience.

title: 16  Numerical Expressions of Uncertainty
text:
Numerical expressions of uncertainty convey the likelihood of an outcome or the margin of error surrounding an estimate through explicit quantitative values — moving beyond the inherent ambiguity of natural language to provide mathematically scalable, verifiable, and aggregable representations of probability. These expressions can take a wide variety of forms: precise point probabilities (a 75% chance), imprecise numeric ranges (a 65–85% chance), fractions (3 in 4), odds (3 to 1 in favour), or natural frequencies such as “1 in 10” or “15 out of 100” (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). By mapping uncertainty onto a 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%) scale, numerical expressions allow degrees of certainty to be quantified, compared, combined, and communicated with a precision that verbal expressions cannot achieve. As Mandel & Irwin (2021) observe, “unlike linguistic probabilities, numeric probabilities have a transparent ordering that users agree on” — a property that is fundamental to their value in decision-making contexts where different sources of uncertain information must be integrated.
Numerical expressions are not a single format but a family of closely related formats, each with distinctive perceptual and cognitive properties. Percentages express probability as a proportion of 100; frequencies express it as a count out of a specified denominator; fractions express it as a ratio of numerator to denominator; odds express it as the ratio of favourable to unfavourable outcomes; and decimal probabilities express it as a number between 0 and 1. These formats are mathematically equivalent — a 10% probability, a 1-in-10 frequency, a 0.10 decimal, and 1-to-9 odds all describe the same underlying quantity — yet the evidence reviewed below demonstrates that they can produce substantially different perceptions, decisions, and levels of comprehension in the same audience. The choice of numerical format is therefore not merely a matter of convention; it is a consequential communication decision.

16.0.1 Practical Example
Numerical expressions of uncertainty are deployed across virtually every domain of quantitative science and risk communication. In meteorology, weather forecasters express the Probability of Precipitation as a percentage — “a 30 percent chance of rain” — in public forecasts that reach millions of people daily (National Research Council, 2006). In intelligence analysis, Mandel & Irwin (2021) advocate replacing vague verbal expressions with numerical credible intervals — for example, reporting a “65% to 85% probability that a given event will occur” — to provide unambiguous, verifiable communication of analytic confidence.
In public health and epidemiology, medical risk calculators communicate chronic disease risk using frequency formats: “12 of 100 people with this level of risk will have a heart attack in the next 10 years” (Spiegelhalter, 2017). This frequency format is specifically chosen because it grounds the abstract probability in a concrete, imaginable group of people rather than an abstract proportion. In food safety, communicators express incremental risks using absolute frequency differences: “one extra case of bowel cancer expected in every 100 people eating processed meat daily” (Spiegelhalter, 2017). This absolute risk format, as discussed further below, is preferred over relative risk expressions precisely because it conveys the actual magnitude of the risk increment without the inflation that relative framing introduces.

16.0.2 Strengths and Limitations
Strengths. Numerical expressions offer four distinctive advantages over verbal alternatives that make them the preferred format for high-stakes uncertainty communication.
The first is unambiguous ordering and transparency. Unlike verbal expressions, which can be interpreted across a 60-percentage-point range for a single term, numerical probabilities have a “transparent ordering that users agree on” (Mandel & Irwin, 2021). A stated probability of 35% is unambiguously lower than one of 60%; there is no analogous consensus about whether “possible” is higher or lower than “maybe.” This ordering property is essential for comparative risk communication, where audiences need to weigh the probability of one outcome against another.
The second advantage is enhanced information processing and aggregation. When decision-making requires the integration of uncertainty information from multiple sources, numerical formats substantially outperform verbal ones. “Arithmetic accuracy and logical coherence of averaging and multiplying probabilities are lower when these are expressed verbally than when expressed as point numeric values” (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). In settings where risk information must be combined — for example, when a patient needs to weigh the probability of benefit against the probability of harm from a treatment — numerical expressions produce more coherent and better-calibrated judgements.
The third advantage is credibility preservation. Communicating uncertainty using numerical ranges or point estimates does not substantially reduce public trust in the source or the numbers themselves (Bles et al., 2020). Senders who make an inaccurate low-probability forecast also suffer less credibility loss if they used a numerical expression than if they used an outcome-incongruent verbal term (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). This evidence directly challenges the common assumption among institutional communicators that expressing numerical uncertainty will damage their credibility.
The fourth advantage is receiver preference for consequential decisions. While senders prefer verbal expressions for their face-saving properties, receivers consistently and strongly prefer numerical precision when they must make high-stakes decisions (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). Crucially, this preference for numbers is not confined to high-numeracy individuals: “when it really matters, both senders and receivers prefer the precise numeric expression of uncertainty” (Dhami & Mandel, 2022).
Limitations. Despite these advantages, numerical expressions are subject to several well-documented biases and limitations that communicators must actively address.
The first is numeracy constraints. A significant proportion of the general public lacks the mathematical training necessary to intuitively process quantitative probability information, which limits the efficacy of purely numerical communication with entry-level audiences (National Research Council, 2006). However, as Dhami & Mandel (2022) note, recent evidence suggests that low-numeracy individuals are not actually harmed by numerical expressions relative to verbal ones — they do not make worse decisions with numbers than with words — and they judge communicators more harshly when vague verbal formats appear to mask errors. The assumption that numbers should be avoided for low-numeracy audiences may therefore be overstated.
The second limitation is reference class ambiguity. Numerical probabilities frequently fail to communicate clearly because audiences misunderstand the implicit denominator. A 60% chance of rain is routinely misinterpreted as meaning rain for 60% of the day’s duration, or rain over 60% of the forecast area, rather than rain on 60% of days with similar meteorological conditions (Spiegelhalter, 2017). This ambiguity is not a property of the number itself but of the failure to specify the reference class — the population of events to which the probability applies.
The third limitation is ratio bias and denominator neglect. When numerical uncertainties are expressed as fractions, human probability perception is heavily biased by the numerator. People incorrectly perceive a risk of 1,286 out of 10,000 as larger than one of 24.14 out of 100, because the absolute size of the numerator (1,286) is larger (Spiegelhalter, 2017). In extreme cases, audiences exhibit “denominator neglect” — focusing entirely on the number of adverse events and ignoring the size of the population from which it is drawn.
The fourth limitation is the 1-in-X magnification effect. Expressing probabilities in a “1-in-X” ratio format systematically inflates the audience’s perception of risk compared to numerically equivalent “N-in-X” formats (Ripberger et al., 2022). A “1 in 33 chance” is perceived as more alarming than a “3 in 100 chance” even though they are mathematically identical.
The fifth limitation is relative risk inflation. Presenting numerical uncertainty as a relative risk reduction — for example, “a 50% decrease in the risk of heart attack” — yields larger, more persuasive numbers than presenting the same information as an absolute risk difference — “a decrease from 8% to 4%” (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). Relative risk framing causes audiences to substantially overestimate the magnitude of the effect, with direct consequences for medical and policy decision-making.
Disagreement in the literature. The most significant and unresolved disagreement in the numerical expressions literature concerns the choice between percentage and frequency formats. Some researchers — notably Gigerenzer and Hoffrage — argue strongly that natural frequencies vastly improve comprehension and Bayesian reasoning, because they align with human experiential learning about counts of events rather than abstract proportions (Spiegelhalter, 2017). A substantial body of research has been developed supporting the frequency advantage. However, contradictory empirical results from Joslyn and Nichols demonstrate that non-expert users actually understood weather forecast uncertainty better when probabilities were presented as percentages rather than frequencies, directly contradicting the frequency advantage hypothesis (Raftery, 2016). Extensive subsequent reviews conclude that there is “no consistent benefit” to either format and that performance depends heavily on the specific task, the population tested, and the context of the communication (Ripberger et al., 2022). This disagreement remains unresolved and argues for empirical testing in specific contexts rather than universal format prescriptions.


16.0.3 When to Use This Method
Numerical expressions should be used as the primary mode of communication whenever the objective is the unambiguous communication of probability to support consequential decision-making (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). They are appropriate across all three audience levels described in Chapter 7, though the specific format chosen — percentage, frequency, odds, or range — should be adapted to the audience’s familiarity and the nature of the decision.
Numerical formats are especially important when the audience is expected to combine uncertainty information from multiple sources, to compare probabilities of different outcomes, or to make a decision that requires trading off asymmetric costs and benefits (Dhami & Mandel, 2022). In these contexts, the cognitive advantages of numerical over verbal formats are most pronounced and most consequential.
Numerical expressions are less appropriate as the primary format when the underlying uncertainty is genuinely unquantifiable — for example, when expressing the pedigree of evidence or the degree of scientific consensus — in which case categorical approaches or verbal expressions of meta-uncertainty are more honest, as discussed in Chapter 13 and Chapter 15.


16.0.4 Good Practice

Use absolute rather than relative risks: To prevent biased, inflated threat perceptions, always express risks and risk changes in absolute rather than relative terms. Present the actual probabilities before and after an intervention, not just the percentage reduction (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). If relative risks are reported at all, always accompany them with the absolute risk difference.
Fix the denominator: When making risk comparisons using frequency formats, always keep the denominator constant across the comparisons — for example, “5 out of 1,000” versus “20 out of 1,000” — rather than allowing the denominator to vary across comparisons (Spiegelhalter, 2017). Varying denominators activate ratio bias and make comparisons unreliable.
Avoid “1-in-X” formats: The “1-in-X” format reliably inflates risk perception and should be replaced by “N-in-X” formats using a consistent base-10 denominator — for example, “3 in 100” rather than “1 in 33” (Ripberger et al., 2022).
Explicitly define the reference class: Every numerical probability must be accompanied by a precise specification of what population, time horizon, and conditions it refers to. “A 30% chance of rain” should be accompanied by a clear statement of what “30%” means in the specific forecasting context (Spiegelhalter, 2017).
Use “% certainty” rather than “% probability” for subjective judgements: Because laypeople associate “probability” with the physical frequency of random events, communicators expressing subjective expert judgements should use terms such as “experts are 90% certain” rather than “there is a 90% probability,” to clarify that the number represents a state of knowledge rather than a population frequency (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Test the chosen format empirically: Given the unresolved frequency- versus-percentage debate and the strong context-dependence of numerical format effects, the appropriate numerical format should be determined through empirical testing with the target audience rather than on the basis of general recommendations (Ripberger et al., 2022).
Provide multiple formats for different audiences: When the same numerical uncertainty must be communicated to audiences with different backgrounds, consider providing the information in multiple formats simultaneously — for example, “10% — that is, 1 in 10” — to maximise the probability that at least one format is well matched to each reader’s prior experience.

title: 17  Conclusion
text:
17.1 Cross-Cutting Principles
This paper has reviewed the conceptual foundations, sources, and communication methods for uncertainty across eight methods and multiple disciplines. What emerges from this review is not a single recommended approach but a set of cross-cutting principles, a framework for matching methods to audiences and contexts, and an honest account of where the empirical foundations of the field remain thin. This concluding section draws together these threads, presents a summary framework for method selection, and identifies the most pressing open questions for future research.
Six principles emerge consistently across the reviewed literature, regardless of the specific method, domain, or audience under consideration.
Principle 1: All prediction is inherently uncertain — communicate it as such. The most fundamental principle is also the most frequently violated. “No forecast is complete without a description of its uncertainty” (National Research Council, 2006), and presenting a point estimate without its associated uncertainty is not a simplification but a misrepresentation. The evidence reviewed in Chapter 6 demonstrates that honest uncertainty communication does not undermine trust — it builds it over time — while the suppression of uncertainty creates institutional fragility that is revealed whenever events diverge from deterministic predictions. Communicating uncertainty is not an admission of weakness; it is a professional and ethical obligation.
Principle 2: Calibration is non-negotiable. Across all eight methods reviewed in Chapter 8, calibration — the property that stated probabilities correspond to observed frequencies — is the foundational requirement for useful uncertainty communication. A method that is visually sophisticated but poorly calibrated provides a misleading picture of uncertainty and may cause greater harm than silence. The gold standard demonstrated by weather forecasters — systematic empirical tracking of forecast performance, continuous feedback, and explicit calibration adjustment — should be adopted as a model across domains (Makridakis & Bakas, 2016).
Principle 3: There is no universally best format — match method to audience and decision. The review of eight communication methods reveals a consistent pattern: every method has genuine strengths in specific contexts and genuine limitations in others. Fan charts are highly informative for expert audiences but overwhelming for the general public. Verbal expressions are natural and accessible but systematically misinterpreted for quantitative probability communication. Numerical frequencies may aid some audiences and hinder others depending on the task. The selection of a communication method must be grounded in a prior understanding of the audience’s statistical literacy, the nature of the decision they face, and the specific type of uncertainty being communicated (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019; Fischhoff & Davis, 2014).
Principle 4: Minimise cognitive load without sacrificing honesty. A consistent finding across all the methods reviewed is that cognitive load is a genuine barrier to effective uncertainty communication. Adding a range to a point estimate triples the cognitive burden; adding a full probability distribution multiplies it further (Raftery, 2016). Communicators should actively seek the simplest format that honestly conveys the decision-relevant aspects of uncertainty, reserving more complex displays for audiences who need and can use them. This is not an argument for oversimplification — it is an argument for deliberate, audience-calibrated design.
Principle 5: Always accompany quantitative displays with qualitative explanation. No graphical or numerical display of uncertainty is self-explanatory. Every display reviewed in Chapter 8 benefits from, and most require, explicit textual accompaniment that defines what the display represents, what the audience should take from it, and — critically — whether it represents epistemic uncertainty about a single unknown quantity or variability across a population (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019). The distinction between epistemic uncertainty and variability, discussed in Chapter 5, is among the most consistently misunderstood aspects of uncertainty communication and must be explicitly addressed in any display that could be confused for a distributional summary of population heterogeneity.
Principle 6: Develop, test, and evaluate. The empirical foundations of uncertainty communication are less settled than practitioners often assume. Multiple direct contradictions between studies have been documented throughout this review — on the utility of CDFs, on the frequency versus percentage debate, on whether verbal expressions help or hurt credibility, on whether more trajectories improve or damage trust. These contradictions argue strongly against the application of any single communication format as a universal best practice. Communication products should be designed for specific audiences and contexts, tested empirically with representative members of those audiences, and evaluated against clear decision-quality criteria before wide deployment (Spiegelhalter, 2017).

Several cross-cutting observations emerge from this summary. First, no method is appropriate for all audience levels: the most informationally complete methods — distributions and fan charts — are appropriate only for technical audiences, while the most accessible methods — categorical approaches and verbal expressions — carry the most significant information loss and interpretive risk. Second, prediction intervals and quantiles occupy a uniquely versatile middle position: they are more informative than categorical or verbal formats while being more accessible than full distributions, making them the most broadly applicable method across audience levels. Third, verbal expressions should never stand alone as a quantitative communication tool, but serve an important function in communicating meta-uncertainty that the quantitative methods cannot express.

title: 17  Conclusion
text:
17.3 Open Questions and Future Research
Despite the breadth of the literature reviewed in this paper, the empirical foundation for understanding how uncertainty communication affects actual decision-making behaviour remains critically underdeveloped. The literature identifies three particularly urgent research gaps.
Behavioural impact beyond comprehension. The large majority of experimental studies on uncertainty communication measure comprehension — whether audiences correctly understand what a display shows — rather than decision quality — whether they make better decisions as a result. These are not the same thing: audiences can understand a probability display correctly yet still fail to integrate it appropriately into their decisions, due to risk aversion, status quo bias, or other cognitive factors. Future research must move beyond measuring understanding to “examine how well test subjects understand uncertainty communication and whether various communication formats result in different decisions” in consequential real-world settings (European Food Safety Authority et al., 2019).
Communicating subjective epistemic judgements to laypeople. The literature has developed relatively clear guidance on communicating aleatory uncertainty — random variation that can be characterised statistically — but offers much less guidance on communicating genuine epistemic uncertainty and deep uncertainty to non-expert audiences. As European Food Safety Authority et al. (2019) observe, there is a “distinct lack of empirical data on how to effectively translate unquantifiable, epistemic uncertainty to laypeople” in a way that does not confuse it for physical randomness or falsely imply a precision that the underlying knowledge does not support. This gap is particularly important given that the most consequential uncertainties in policy — about climate tipping points, novel pathogens, emerging technologies — are precisely the ones that cannot be quantified with confidence.
Systematic experimental manipulation of communication components. Because the existing literature is widely scattered across disciplines, contexts, and audiences, it is difficult to draw firm comparative conclusions about which format features — visual encoding, numerical format, verbal labelling, level of detail, framing — drive comprehension and decision outcomes. Bles et al. (2019) call for future research to “manipulate components of communication systematically in order to unpick their effects and identify mediators” through controlled experimental designs. Such research would allow practitioners to move from discipline-specific case studies to generalisable design principles applicable across the full range of uncertainty communication contexts.

title: 17  Conclusion
text:
17.4 Closing Remarks
Uncertainty is not the enemy of good decision-making — it is its necessary context. Every forecast, projection, and scientific estimate is produced under conditions of incomplete knowledge, and the honest representation of that incompleteness is a prerequisite for the kind of calibrated, adaptive decision-making that complex challenges require. The eight methods reviewed in this paper are not substitutes for each other but complementary tools, each suited to specific audiences, specific types of uncertainty, and specific decision contexts. No single method is best; the skill lies in choosing wisely, explaining clearly, and testing honestly.
The literature reviewed in this paper also makes clear that the challenge of uncertainty communication is not merely technical. It is institutional — requiring organisations to reward honest uncertainty disclosure rather than penalising it (Fischhoff & Davis, 2014). It is cultural — requiring communicators and their audiences to develop a shared vocabulary and shared norms for probabilistic thinking. And it is ethical — requiring that the needs of the audience and the quality of their decisions take precedence over the comfort and face-saving preferences of the communicator.
The greatest barrier to good uncertainty communication is not the absence of good methods. It is the persistent temptation — felt by scientists, forecasters, institutions, and policymakers alike — to project a confidence that the evidence does not warrant. Overcoming this temptation, and building the institutional and cultural infrastructure to support honest uncertainty communication at scale, is among the most important and underinvested challenges in the practice of science, policy, and public communication.
